```mermaid
    L->>T: Celebrate first visible success
    T-->>E: Confidence and momentum
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define "Belief Changers" in the context of change management. Why are they considered more effective than pure information-sharing?

**Model Answer:**
Belief Changers are deliberate leadership interventions that alter *what people believe* about a change — its relevance, its emotional meaning, its originator's trustworthiness, its future promise, its leaders' commitment, and its early results. They are more effective than pure information-sharing because human decisions are driven primarily by *belief, emotion, and social proof* rather than by data alone. Information updates the *mind*; belief changers update the *heart and identity*, which is what sustains long-term behavioural change. *(3 marks — definition 1 + explanation 2)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** List any three of the six belief changers and state the specific belief each one produces in employees.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Personalization** → Belief produced: *"This change is relevant to me personally."*
2. **Emotional Connection** → Belief produced: *"I feel this change is right."*
3. **Small Wins** → Belief produced: *"This change actually works."*
*(1 mark each, 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Six Belief Changers** that influence change in an organization. For each changer, identify the specific belief it creates and the psychological principle behind it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Six Belief Changers to a **real engineering scenario** of your choice (e.g., implementing ERP, lean manufacturing, Industry 4.0, or safety culture change). Diagnose what would go wrong if any one of the six changers were missing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

**(a) — The Six Belief Changers (7 marks):**

| # | Belief Changer | Belief Produced | Psychological Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Personalization | "It matters to me" | Self-referential processing |
| 2 | Emotional Connection | "I feel it" | Affect heuristic / narrative transportation |
| 3 | Trust & Credibility | "I trust them" | Source credibility theory |
| 4 | Compelling Vision | "Future is worth it" | Future-oriented motivation |
| 5 | Role Modelling | "Leaders live it" | Social learning theory (Bandura) |
| 6 | Small Wins | "Change works" | Reinforcement & momentum |

Each row contributes approximately **1 mark**. The integration statement — *"Belief precedes behaviour; information alone is insufficient"* — secures the remaining **1 mark**.

**(b) — Application to ERP Implementation in a Manufacturing Company (7 marks):**

- *Personalization:* Operators are shown how ERP eliminates 2 hours of daily manual reporting → belief: "It frees me for skilled work." **(1 mark)**
- *Emotional Connection:* A peer operator from another plant shares how ERP helped her become a process analyst → belief: "It changed someone's life." **(1 mark)**
- *Trust & Credibility:* CIO admits that the previous system failed because of poor leadership, not because of IT → belief: "They are honest now." **(1 mark)**
- *Compelling Vision:* 3D walkthrough of the connected factory of 2028 → belief: "I can see where we are going." **(1 mark)**
- *Role Modelling:* Plant head personally logs into ERP every morning during the pilot → belief: "If she uses it, it must be real." **(1 mark)**
- *Small Wins:* First production line reduces inventory error by 40% in 60 days; team is publicly rewarded → belief: "It works here." **(1 mark)**
- **Diagnostic conclusion:** Without *Trust*, the past-failure story resurfaces and ERP is labelled "another IT fashion." Without *Small Wins*, mid-project fatigue kills momentum. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

**(a)** "Information alone does not change behaviour; belief does." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to the **Six Belief Changers** framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast Kotter's **Six Belief Changers** with his earlier **Eight-Step Change Model**. How do belief changers *complement* the eight steps? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

**(a) — Critical Evaluation (7 marks):**
- The statement is **true** in most organizational contexts. **(1 mark)**
- Information creates *awareness*; belief creates *commitment*. **(1 mark)**
- Kotter's research shows over **70%** of change failures are rooted in under-addressing the human/belief dimension. **(1 mark)**
- Each of the six belief changers addresses a specific *psychological barrier* (cynicism, fear, inertia, scepticism). **(2 marks)**
- **Counter-view:** In *highly regulated or compliance-driven* contexts (e.g., aviation safety, pharma GMP), information *plus authority* can drive behaviour without deep belief change — but the change remains fragile. **(1 mark)**
- **Conclusion:** Belief-based change is more durable; information-based compliance is more fragile. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Comparison with Eight-Step Model (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Eight-Step Model | Six Belief Changers |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process / sequence of actions | Psychological levers operating *within* the process |
| Nature | Linear roadmap | Parallel human levers |
| Origin | Strategic planning mindset | Human/behavioural mindset |
| Strength | Tells you *what to do* | Tells you *how to make people believe it* |
| Weakness | Can be followed mechanically | Requires emotional intelligence |

- The eight steps tell the leader **what** to do at each stage; belief changers tell the leader **how** to make each stage *land in human hearts*. **(2 marks)**
- Example: *Step 4 (Communicate Vision)* maps to *Belief Changer 4 (Vision)* — but the belief changer adds the *emotional* and *personal* layer that the eight-step alone may miss. **(2 marks)**
- Conclusion: The two frameworks are **complementary, not competing**. Belief changers are the *psychological engine* of the eight-step machine. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Listing without linking** — Writing "Personalization, Emotion, Trust…" without explaining *what belief each produces* costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **No example** — A 14-mark answer without a real workplace scenario is capped at approximately **9 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring sequence** — Treating the six as a random checklist rather than as a *diagnostic-then-deployment* sequence loses marks on the "process" component.
> 4. **Confusing belief changers with eight steps** — They are *complementary frameworks*. Examiners actively check whether the student can distinguish them.
> 5. **Forgetting the human element** — An answer that sounds like a *machine manual* rather than a *human narrative* is a strong signal of rote memorization and is marked strictly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Belief Changers — Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- **Belief Changers** = psychological levers that change *what people believe*, not just *what they know*.
- They work on the principle that **belief precedes behaviour**.
- The six changers are: **Personalization, Emotional Connection, Trust & Credibility, Compelling Vision, Role Modelling, Small Wins**.
- **Personalization** → speaks to *self-interest*; uses one-on-one dialogue.
- **Emotional Connection** → speaks to *meaning*; uses stories, not data.
- **Trust & Credibility** → speaks to *safety*; built through honesty + track record.
- **Compelling Vision** → speaks to *hope*; paints the future vividly.
- **Role Modelling** → speaks to *legitimacy*; leaders *do* what they ask.
- **Small Wins** → speaks to *confidence*; celebrates quick, visible progress.
- Each changer addresses a specific *psychological barrier* to change.
- They **complement** Kotter's Eight-Step Model — they are *not* a replacement.
- Failure to deploy them results in **change resistance, cynicism, and project abandonment**.
- In engineering contexts (ERP, lean, Industry 4.0, safety), they are the **human factor** that determines success or failure.
- KTU 2024 Scheme tests this topic primarily at CO3 (*Apply change management strategies*) and CO4 (*Evaluate leadership interventions*).
- Always answer with **definition → psychological basis → workplace example → diagnostic consequence**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organisational Change Through Influencing Individual Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Organisational Change Through Influencing Individual Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organisational Change Through Influencing Individual Change** is a foundational principle in change management that asserts sustainable organisational transformation cannot be achieved by altering structures, systems, or strategies alone. Instead, change must be enacted at the **micro level of the individual employee** because every organisation is ultimately a collective of individuals whose behaviours, attitudes, beliefs, and competencies determine aggregate performance.

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this concept using three interlocking pillars:

1. **Individual-level transition** (cognitive, emotional, behavioural) — the inner shift a person undergoes when asked to "do things differently."
2. **Leader-as-change-agent** — the manager's role in diagnosing readiness, reducing resistance, and creating conditions for voluntary adoption.
3. **Aggregation effect** — when individual changes are aligned, repeated, and reinforced, they emerge as organisation-wide culture, productivity, and strategy execution.

The **premise** is captured in the classical equation:
$$\text{Organisational Change} \;=\; f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Individual Change}_i\right)$$
where *n* equals the number of employees meaningfully engaged in the change. If the sum equals zero, no structural reform will yield real transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO3) of PESFT634 explicitly maps to "Apply behavioural principles to influence individual change within organisational contexts." Every board exam question on this topic expects students to **link a specific individual-level intervention to a measurable organisational outcome**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River and the Boats

Imagine an organisation as a **fleet of 500 boats** drifting down a river. The CEO wants all boats to change direction and head east (the new strategy). Two options exist:

- **Option A (Structural Only):** Re-draw the map, print new banners, and announce the eastward directive from a podium. Most boats keep drifting south because *their captains (employees) did not personally turn their rudders.*
- **Option B (Individual-Level Change):** Walk to each captain, explain *why* east matters (purpose), teach them *how* to steer (capability), address their fears of storms in the eastern route (emotion), and reward the first few captains who turn (reinforcement). Within weeks, the *fleet* visibly redirects.

**Individual change is the rudder; organisational change is the fleet's heading.** No fleet turns if no rudder turns.

---

## 1.3 Why Individual Change is the *Primary* Lever

| # | Reason | Micro-Level Reality | Macro-Level Outcome |
|---|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Behaviour lives in people | Every process, decision, and customer interaction is performed by a human | Strategy execution fails without behaviour change |
| 2 | Culture = aggregate habits | Individual routines compound into shared norms | Culture shifts when individuals adopt new norms |
| 3 | Knowledge resides in minds | Skills, tacit knowledge, and insights are individual assets | Innovation requires individual learning |
| 4 | Resistance originates internally | Fear, loss, and uncertainty are personal experiences | Organisational inertia is the sum of individual fears |
| 5 | Engagement is voluntary | Commitment cannot be mandated; only invited | Sustained performance requires individual will |

> [!TIP]
> Use the **5-R Principle** in your answer: *Reasons, Routines, Resistance, Reinforcement, Results* — every individual change intervention must touch all five to succeed at scale.

---

## 1.4 The Three Layers of Change (Syllabus Anchor)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 3: ORGANISATIONAL CHANGE                          │
│   (Strategy • Structure • Systems • Culture)             │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  LAYER 2: TEAM / GROUP CHANGE                    │    │
│  │   (Norms • Roles • Coordination)                  │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │  │  LAYER 1: INDIVIDUAL CHANGE              │    │    │
│  │  │   (Mindset • Skill • Behaviour • Will)   │    │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The KTU board examiner expects students to argue that **Layer 1 is the foundation**. Without it, Layers 2 and 3 are hollow redesigns that produce no real-world shift.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lewin's Three-Stage Model — The Operating System

Kurt Lewin (1947) proposed that individual change moves through three sequential stages. This is the **canonical model** that nearly every KTU board question references.

### Stage 1: UNFREEZE
**Goal:** Create motivation to change by breaking the current equilibrium.

**Why it matters:** Humans are wired for homeostasis. Without disrupting the comfort zone, no change energy is released.

**How a leader executes it:**
- Communicate a *burning platform* — explain why status quo is no longer safe.
- Surface dissatisfaction with current performance.
- Share compelling vision of the future state.
- Diagnose and acknowledge individual anxieties.

### Stage 2: CHANGE (Transition)
**Goal:** Enable new behaviours, beliefs, and skills through learning and role-modelling.

**Why it matters:** Even motivated employees need capability + safe space to practice the new way.

**How a leader executes it:**
- Provide training, coaching, mentoring.
- Encourage small experiments and tolerate mistakes.
- Identify and empower change champions.
- Communicate frequently to maintain momentum.

### Stage 3: REFREEZE
**Goal:** Stabilise and institutionalise the new behaviour so it becomes the new normal.

**Why it matters:** Without reinforcement, employees revert to old habits within 60–90 days (a finding well-documented in change-management literature).

**How a leader executes it:**
- Update policies, reward systems, and KPIs to reflect new behaviours.
- Celebrate quick wins publicly.
- Anchor changes in organisational culture and onboarding.
- Monitor and audit for regression.

---

## 2.2 Bridges' Transition Model — The Emotional Journey

William Bridges (1991) argued that it is not *change* people resist, but **transition** — the psychological reorientation. He identified three phases:

| Phase | Internal Experience | Leader's Role |
|-------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Ending, Losing, Letting Go** | Denial, sadness, fear, bargaining | Acknowledge loss, communicate openly, allow grieving |
| **The Neutral Zone** | Confusion, anxiety, low morale, creativity | Provide structure, short-term wins, coaching |
| **New Beginning** | Acceptance, energy, commitment | Reinforce identity, celebrate milestones, embed new roles |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exam tip: If the question mentions "employees are anxious and unproductive 3 months into a change," the answer is almost certainly *Neutral Zone* — describe it and prescribe short-term wins + structured communication.

---

## 2.3 The ADKAR Model (Prosci) — Five Building Blocks of Individual Change

This is the **most testable** framework in KTU 2024 exams. ADKAR is an acronym where each letter must be present for change to occur:

| Letter | Element | Individual Question | Failure Symptom |
|--------|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **A** | Awareness | Why must we change? | "I don't see the problem." |
| **D** | Desire | Do I want to change? | "I see the need, but I won't." |
| **K** | Knowledge | How do I change? | "I want to, but I don't know how." |
| **A** | Ability | Can I apply the new skill? | "I trained but cannot perform." |
| **R** | Reinforcement | Will I be rewarded for sustaining it? | "I changed but went back to old habits." |

**KTU formula derivation:**
$$\text{Individual Change Success} = A \times D \times K \times A \times R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicative, not additive.** If any one element equals zero, the entire product collapses. Awareness × Desire × Knowledge × Ability × Reinforcement = 0 if, say, Reinforcement is missing.

---

## 2.4 Kotter's 8 Steps Applied to Individual Influence

While Kotter's model is organisation-wide, KTU examiners frequently ask students to **map each step to the individual level**:

| Kotter Step | Individual Influence Action |
|------------|----------------------------|
| 1. Create urgency | One-on-one conversations about personal stakes |
| 2. Form coalition | Identify peer influencers who adopt first |
| 3. Vision | Connect the change to the individual's purpose |
| 4. Communicate | Repeated, multi-channel, two-way dialogue |
| 5. Empower | Remove personal barriers; provide resources |
| 6. Short-term wins | Personal milestone recognition |
| 7. Consolidate | Build on initial change with deeper adoption |
| 8. Anchor | Integrate new behaviour into performance review |

---

## 2.5 Sources of Individual Resistance

| Source | Underlying Fear | Counter-Intervention |
|--------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Economic** | Job loss, demotion, pay cut | Transparent job-security communication |
| **Psychological** | Loss of competence, identity | Coaching, retraining, reassurance |
| **Social** | Loss of group status | Inclusive coalition-building |
| **Habitual** | Comfort with routine | Gradual transition, small steps |
| **Information** | Misunderstanding of goal | Clear, repeated messaging |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of the Individual-Change Lens

- **HR & L&D:** Designing onboarding, leadership development, and capability-building programmes.
- **Operations Management:** Sustaining Lean, Six Sigma, and TQM gains (which fail ~70% of the time due to individual reversion).
- **Mergers & Acquisitions:** Overcoming the "merger of equals" failure where cultures clash at the individual level.
- **Digital Transformation:** Closing the technology-adoption gap where new tools are installed but old workflows persist.
- **Performance Management:** Translating strategy into personal goals and habits.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Why ADKAR is Multiplicative

### Step 1: Define each ADKAR element as a binary state variable.

Let us define for any individual *i* at time *t*:

- $A_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — Awareness present (1) or absent (0)
- $D_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — Desire present or absent
- $K_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — Knowledge present or absent
- $A_{b,i}(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — Ability present or absent
- $R_i(t) \in \{0,1\}$ — Reinforcement present or absent

### Step 2: Establish the necessary and sufficient condition.

For an individual to *sustain* a new behaviour, all five elements must be present *simultaneously*. Logic dictates:

$$\text{Behaviour Sustained}_i \iff (A_i = 1) \land (D_i = 1) \land (K_i = 1) \land (A_{b,i} = 1) \land (R_i = 1)$$

### Step 3: Convert conjunction into multiplication.

For binary variables, logical AND is mathematically equivalent to multiplication:

$$S_i = A_i \times D_i \times K_i \times A_{b,i} \times R_i$$

Where $S_i$ is the **sustained-change score** for individual *i*.

### Step 4: Demonstrate the multiplicative consequence.

Suppose an organisation trains 1,000 employees ($K = 1$ for all), but reinforcement is missing ($R = 0$).

$$S_i = 1 \times 1 \times 1 \times 1 \times 0 = 0$$

**Result:** Even though knowledge is perfect, *zero* individuals sustain the change. This is why **80% of training programmes fail to change behaviour** — the reinforcement loop is broken.

### Step 5: Extend to the organisational level.

$$\text{Org Change Success} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(A_i \cdot D_i \cdot K_i \cdot A_{b,i} \cdot R_i)$$

If 60% of employees have Awareness, 50% Desire, 80% Knowledge, 70% Ability, but only 30% Reinforcement, then:

$$S_{org} = 0.60 \times 0.50 \times 0.80 \times 0.70 \times 0.30 = 0.0504 \;(5.04\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 5% explains why so many corporate change initiatives "die on the vine." The bottleneck is rarely awareness or knowledge — it is **desire and reinforcement**.

---

## 3.2 Force Field Analysis — Derivation of Change Energy

Lewin modelled human behaviour as a quasi-static equilibrium of driving and restraining forces.

### Step 1: Define the status-quo equilibrium.

Let $E_d$ = total driving force and $E_r$ = total restraining force. Equilibrium holds when:

$$\sum F = E_d - E_r = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_d = E_r$$

### Step 2: Define the change condition.

For individual movement toward the new state, driving forces must exceed restraining forces by a margin exceeding the individual's **inertia threshold** $\theta$:

$$E_d - E_r > \theta_i \quad \text{(for individual } i\text{)}$$

### Step 3: Leader's strategic options.

A leader has three levers:
1. **Increase** $E_d$ (e.g., incentives, vision, urgency).
2. **Decrease** $E_r$ (e.g., training to reduce fear, removing barriers).
3. **Lower** $\theta_i$ (e.g., building trust, psychological safety).

$$\Delta S_{success} = \max_{strategy}\left[(E_d + \Delta E_d) - (E_r - \Delta E_r) - (\theta_i - \Delta \theta_i)\right]$$

---

## 3.3 Rogers' Diffusion of Innovations — The S-Curve Derivation

### Step 1: Categorise the population by adoption time.

- **Innovators** — 2.5%
- **Early Adopters** — 13.5%
- **Early Majority** — 34%
- **Late Majority** — 34%
- **Laggards** — 16%

### Step 2: Cumulative adoption follows a logistic function.

$$P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = carrying capacity (typically 100% for sustained change)
- $k$ = steepness of adoption
- $t_0$ = inflection point (when 50% have adopted)
- $t$ = time

### Step 3: Critical mass threshold.

Sustainable organisational change typically requires the **Early Majority** to commit, which Rogers identified at:

$$P_{critical} \approx 0.16 \;\text{(16% adoption — end of Early Adopters)}$$

Until this threshold is reached, the change remains fragile.

---

## 3.4 Behavioural Persistence Over Time — Decay Function

### Step 1: Empirical observation.

Studies show that newly trained behaviours decay exponentially without reinforcement.

$$P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 2: Interpretation.

- $P_0$ = initial proficiency immediately post-training
- $\lambda$ = decay constant (typical 0.02–0.05 per week for soft skills)
- $t$ = time in weeks

### Step 3: Refreeze strength index.

A robust refreeze stage lowers $\lambda$ dramatically. Best-in-class organisations achieve:

$$\lambda_{refrozen} \approx 0.005 \;\text{per week} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{26\,wk} \approx 0.88\,P_0$$

Whereas weak refreeze yields:

$$\lambda_{weak} \approx 0.04 \;\text{per week} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{26\,wk} \approx 0.35\,P_0$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Linking Interventions to Individual Change Outcomes

| Leader Intervention | Lewin Stage | ADKAR Element | Bridges' Phase | Expected Behavioural Outcome |
|--------------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| Town hall with Q&A | Unfreeze | Awareness | Ending | Reduced denial; questions emerge |
| One-on-one coaching | Change | Knowledge + Ability | Neutral Zone | Skill acquisition, confidence |
| Recognition ceremony | Refreeze | Reinforcement | New Beginning | Sustained new behaviour |
| Pilot project team | Unfreeze + Change | Desire + Knowledge | Neutral Zone | Peer influence, reduced fear |
| Revised performance review | Refreeze | Reinforcement | New Beginning | Cultural embedding |
| Safe-space forums | Unfreeze | Desire | Ending | Emotional processing, buy-in |
| Mentorship pairing | Change | Ability | Neutral Zone | Faster skill transfer |
| Storytelling from CEO | Unfreeze | Awareness + Desire | Ending | Emotional resonance, urgency |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Diagnosing a Failed Change

### Scenario:
A Kerala-based IT firm rolled out a new agile methodology to 500 developers. After 6 months:
- 90% attended training (Knowledge ✓)
- 70% understood why (Awareness ✓)
- 30% wanted to change (Desire ✗)
- 25% could actually do it (Ability ✗)
- 5% saw any recognition (Reinforcement ✗)

### Step 1: Apply ADKAR.

$$S_{org} = 0.90 \times 0.30 \times 0.90 \times 0.25 \times 0.05$$

$$S_{org} = 0.00304 \;=\; 0.30\%$$

### Step 2: Diagnosis.

The bottleneck is **Desire + Reinforcement**. Training (Knowledge) is not the problem.

### Step 3: Recommended interventions.

1. Manager-led conversations to surface and address fears → boost **Desire**.
2. Update incentive structures and celebrate scrum-master achievements → boost **Reinforcement**.
3. Pair struggling developers with Agile champions → boost **Ability**.

### Step 4: Forecasted outcome after 90 days of intervention.

$$S_{org}^{new} = 0.95 \times 0.65 \times 0.95 \times 0.60 \times 0.55$$

$$S_{org}^{new} = 0.1931 \;=\; 19.3\%$$

A **64-fold improvement** by fixing the weakest ADKAR elements.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"Change must occur at the individual level before it can occur at the organisational level." Critically discuss in the context of the ADKAR model. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ADKAR model (Prosci) identifies five sequential building blocks that an individual must possess for change to occur: **A**wareness of the need, **D**esire to support, **K**nowledge of how to change, **A**bility to implement, and **R**einforcement to sustain. The model is multiplicative — a missing element causes the entire change to fail, regardless of strength in other elements. Therefore, organisational change is *fundamentally* an aggregate of successful individual transitions. Strategy, structure, and systems only enable behaviour; they cannot create it. A leader who invests only in training (Knowledge) but ignores Desire or Reinforcement will see the change fade, demonstrating that *individual-level success is the prerequisite for organisational-level success*. (3 marks — 1 for each of: model identification, multiplicative logic, organisational implication)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain William Bridges' Transition Model and how it differs from Lewin's Change Model. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bridges' Transition Model** is a *psychological* model focused on the **internal emotional journey** of an individual through three phases: (1) *Ending, Losing, Letting Go* — acknowledging what must be given up; (2) *The Neutral Zone* — a chaotic but creative in-between state; and (3) *New Beginning* — acceptance and renewed energy.

**Lewin's Change Model** is a *behavioural* model focused on **the process of moving** an individual or group through three stages: *Unfreeze*, *Change*, and *Refreeze*.

**Key difference:** Lewin describes *what must be done* (process), while Bridges describes *what the person experiences* (psychology). Lewin is action-oriented; Bridges is emotion-oriented. Together they offer a complete picture — leaders design interventions per Lewin while empathising with employees per Bridges. (3 marks — 1 mark each for Bridges, Lewin, and the distinction)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Modified]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **ADKAR model** with its five elements. Discuss how a manager can use this model to diagnose and overcome resistance to change at the individual level. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* "Sustainable organisational change is impossible without successful individual change." Critically analyse this statement using **Lewin's Force Field Analysis**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — ADKAR Model (7 marks)

The **ADKAR model**, developed by Jeff Hiatt (Prosci), is a results-oriented change management framework that operates at the individual level. It posits that organisational change happens one person at a time, and each person must progress through five sequential outcomes.

**The Five Elements (5 marks):**

1. **A — Awareness of the Need for Change**
The individual understands *why* the change is necessary. This includes awareness of the business problem, risks of not changing, and the vision for the future. Without awareness, the individual sees no reason to participate.

2. **D — Desire to Support and Participate**
The individual *wants* to be part of the change. This is personal and emotional — it answers the question, "What's in it for me, and do I want to make this shift?" Desire is the most fragile element because it is influenced by personal circumstances, trust in leadership, and fear of loss.

3. **K — Knowledge of How to Change**
The individual knows *what* to do differently — the new behaviours, processes, tools, and skills. This includes training, education, and access to information.

4. **A — Ability to Implement the Change**
The individual can *actually perform* the new behaviour in real conditions. This moves beyond knowing to doing. Common barriers include time, resources, prior habits, and inadequate practice opportunities.

5. **R — Reinforcement to Sustain the Change**
The individual receives recognition, rewards, and accountability that lock in the new behaviour. Without reinforcement, behaviour decays back to the old way.

**Diagnosis and Overcoming Resistance (2 marks):**

A manager applies ADKAR by *interviewing* and *surveying* employees to identify which element is failing. If the diagnostic shows low Awareness, the manager invests in communication. If low Desire, the manager invests in personal conversations, involvement, and addressing fear. If low Knowledge, training is enhanced. If low Ability, coaching and practice opportunities are added. If low Reinforcement, recognition and reward systems are redesigned. Because the model is multiplicative, fixing the weakest element yields the largest gains.

---

#### Part (b) — Force Field Analysis Critique (7 marks)

**Lewin's Force Field Analysis (3 marks):**

Kurt Lewin conceptualised human behaviour as a quasi-equilibrium between **driving forces** (which push toward change) and **restraining forces** (which maintain the status quo). Change occurs when driving forces exceed restraining forces beyond an individual's *inertia threshold*. The leader's task is twofold: amplify drivers (vision, incentives, urgency) and diminish restraints (fear, skill gaps, habits).

**Application to the Statement (2 marks):**

The statement that *sustainable organisational change is impossible without successful individual change* is **largely true** under Force Field Analysis. Every driving force (e.g., a new strategic vision) and every restraining force (e.g., an employee's fear of redundancy) operates *inside an individual mind*. The organisational equilibrium is the *aggregate* of individual equilibria. Therefore, no matter how powerful the driving forces at the macro level, if restraining forces within each individual remain dominant, no net change occurs. Conversely, when individuals shift, the organisational equilibrium shifts automatically.

**Critical Counter-Perspective (2 marks):**

However, the statement can be challenged. Structural changes (e.g., new IT systems, redesigned workflows) can *force* individual change by removing the option of the old behaviour. For instance, when a company migrates to a new ERP, employees must adopt the new system because the old one is decommissioned. In such cases, organisational change *enables and compels* individual change rather than the reverse. Furthermore, culture change through symbolic acts (e.g., a new dress code, redesigned office layout) can produce individual behavioural shifts without each individual consciously "changing."

**Conclusion:** Individual change is the *foundation*, but in modern organisations it is often *catalysed* by structural and systemic organisational change. The relationship is bidirectional, though individual change remains the primary determinant of sustained transformation.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Modified]**
*(a)* Describe **Lewin's Three-Stage Model** of change. Apply it to a real-world scenario of your choice and explain how a leader can ensure each stage succeeds. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the concept of **psychological contract** in organisations. How does its violation trigger individual resistance, and what role does the manager play in restoring it during change? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Lewin's Three-Stage Model (7 marks)

**The Three Stages (3 marks):**

Kurt Lewin's model proposes that change happens through three stages: **Unfreeze**, **Change**, and **Refreeze**.

- **Unfreeze:** The existing equilibrium is disrupted. The individual or group must let go of old behaviours, beliefs, and structures. This requires creating a compelling reason to change (burning platform), surfacing dissatisfaction with the status quo, and communicating a clear vision of the future.
- **Change (Transition):** Once unfrozen, the individual enters a learning phase. New behaviours, attitudes, and skills are introduced through training, coaching, role-modelling, and experimentation. This is the most vulnerable phase — confusion, anxiety, and temporary performance dips are common.
- **Refreeze:** The new behaviour is stabilised and integrated into the organisational system. Policies, reward structures, performance metrics, and cultural norms are aligned with the change to lock it in permanently.

**Real-World Scenario — Implementation of a New Performance Management System at a Manufacturing Firm (2 marks):**

- **Unfreeze:** The CEO shares data showing declining productivity, announces that the current system is outdated, and invites employees to participate in shaping the new system.
- **Change:** HR runs workshops, pilot programmes in two plants, identifies change champions, and provides managers with coaching on giving continuous feedback.
- **Refreeze:** The new system is linked to bonuses, embedded in onboarding for new hires, and quarterly reviews track adoption. After 12 months, the new system becomes "the way we do things."

**Leader's Role in Each Stage (2 marks):**

- In **Unfreeze**, the leader communicates urgency and provides psychological safety to admit that the current state is unsustainable.
- In **Change**, the leader role-models the new behaviour, removes obstacles, and celebrates small wins to maintain momentum.
- In **Refreeze**, the leader institutionalises the change through systems and symbols (e.g., updated reward structures, recognition programmes, revised policies).

---

#### Part (b) — Psychological Contract (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**

The **psychological contract** refers to the unwritten set of mutual expectations, beliefs, and obligations between an employee and the employer. Unlike the formal employment contract, it covers intangible aspects such as loyalty, fair treatment, development opportunities, job security, and a supportive work environment.

**Violation and Resistance (3 marks):**

When employees perceive that the organisation has broken the psychological contract — for example, by suddenly announcing layoffs, freezing promotions, or shifting to a culture of overwork without consultation — they experience **breach**, which manifests as emotional responses (anger, betrayal, sadness) and behavioural responses (reduced effort, disengagement, absenteeism, turnover). In change contexts, perceived violation is one of the strongest drivers of resistance.

**Manager's Role in Restoration (2 marks):**

The manager plays a critical role in either *preventing* or *repairing* breach by:
1. Communicating transparently about decisions and their rationale.
2. Involving employees in the design of the change where possible.
3. Acknowledging past commitments and explaining why adjustments are needed.
4. Offering tangible trade-offs (training, redeployment, new opportunities) to restore the sense of fairness.
5. Following through consistently — rebuilding trust requires repeated reliable actions over time.

**Conclusion:** A healthy psychological contract is the *invisible infrastructure* of individual willingness to change. Managers who honour it cultivate resilient, adaptive employees; those who breach it cultivate disengagement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Lewin and Bridges.** Lewin is *process* (Unfreeze-Change-Refreeze); Bridges is *experience* (Ending-Neutral-New Beginning). Markers deduct 1–2 marks for conflating them.
> 2. **Treating ADKAR as additive instead of multiplicative.** Writing *"we achieved 4 out of 5 elements"* misses the entire point. It is *all or nothing* — a single zero collapses the product.
> 3. **Forgetting to link individual change to organisational change.** Every answer must end with an *aggregation statement*: "When this is multiplied across the workforce, organisational change follows."
> 4. **Omitting emotional / psychological dimension.** KTU 2024 values the human side. Pure structural answers are capped at 60–70% of marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Refreeze.** A common pitfall is to discuss Unfreeze and Change in detail but skip Refreeze, causing behaviour to decay. Always close with institutionalisation.
> 6. **Using only one framework.** Best answers triangulate Lewin + ADKAR + Bridges + Rogers to demonstrate integrated thinking.
> 7. **Forgetting real-world examples.** Kerala-context examples (e.g., KMRL change, Infosys Kochi transformation, Kerala Startup Mission) earn higher marks than abstract ones.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Lewin's Model:** Unfreeze → Change → Refreeze (process orientation).
- [ ] **Bridges' Transition:** Ending → Neutral Zone → New Beginning (psychological orientation).
- [ ] **ADKAR:** Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement — **multiplicative**.
- [ ] **Force Field Analysis:** Driving Forces vs. Restraining Forces; equilibrium model.
- [ ] **Rogers' Diffusion:** Innovators (2.5%), Early Adopters (13.5%), Early Majority (34%), Late Majority (34%), Laggards (16%).
- [ ] **Critical Mass:** ~16% adoption needed for sustained diffusion.
- [ ] **Behavioural Decay:** New behaviours decay exponentially without reinforcement ($P(t) = P_0 e^{-\lambda t}$).
- [ ] **Resistance Sources:** Economic, Psychological, Social, Habitual, Informational.
- [ ] **Psychological Contract:** Unwritten mutual expectations; breach triggers resistance.
- [ ] **Individual → Team → Organisation:** Pyramid of aggregation; change is emergent.
- [ ] **5-R Principle:** Reasons, Routines, Resistance, Reinforcement, Results.
- [ ] **Kotter's 8 Steps** can be mapped to individual-level interventions.
- [ ] **Refreeze Levers:** Policies, rewards, KPIs, onboarding, culture.
- [ ] **80/20 Rule:** ~80% of change initiatives fail due to weak individual-level adoption.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 CO3 Mapping:** Apply behavioural principles to influence individual change.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *An organisation changes when its people change. The leader's job is to unfreeze the old self, walk alongside the new self, and refreeze the new self into the culture.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches: Kotter's Eight Step Plan, Greiner's Change Process Model
Four Key Drivers of Organisational Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Four Key Drivers of Organisational Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Four Key Drivers of Organisational Change** are the four fundamental internal and external forces that compel an organisation to transform its existing state of operations, strategy, culture, or structure. According to standard management literature and the KTU 2024 leadership syllabus, these drivers are **Technology, People, Structure, and External Environment (Task Environment)**.

These drivers act as the **catalysts** that disrupt the equilibrium of an organisation, forcing leadership to initiate planned or unplanned change responses. In the context of the **PESTEL framework** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal), the four internal/external drivers cover both micro and macro forces that shape the need for transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Understanding the four key drivers is foundational to diagnosing why change is necessary. Each driver interacts with leadership behaviour, and together they determine the speed, scale, and success of any change initiative. A change agent (leader) must identify which driver is dominant before prescribing a change strategy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an organisation as a **four-engine passenger aeroplane** cruising at 35,000 feet:

- **Engine 1 – Technology:** The fuel injection system (new digital tools, AI, automation).
- **Engine 2 – People:** The pilots and cabin crew (workforce, skills, attitudes, culture).
- **Engine 3 – Structure:** The airframe and wiring (hierarchy, reporting lines, workflows, departments).
- **Engine 4 – External Environment:** The weather and air traffic (competition, regulations, customer demand, economic conditions).

If **any one engine falters**, the plane starts shaking. If **all four engines malfunction**, the plane cannot sustain flight. The **pilot (leader)** must constantly monitor these four engines and make adjustments (change) to keep the flight on course. This is precisely how the four drivers function in an organisation.

---

## 1.3 The Four Drivers – One-Line Snapshot

| Driver | Core Nature | Primary Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Technology** | Tools, systems, automation, digital transformation | Internal (adoption) & External (innovation) |
| **People** | Workforce, leadership, culture, skills | Internal (predominantly) |
| **Structure** | Hierarchy, design, processes, control systems | Internal (predominantly) |
| **External Environment** | Market, competition, regulation, customer trends | External |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Referenced in KTU:** The **"Change Readiness Index (CRI)"** — a conceptual measure used to assess how prepared an organisation is for change, determined by scoring each of the four drivers on parameters like *urgency, capacity, and willingness*. The CRI is typically expressed as a percentage:
> 
> **CRI (%) = (Sum of Driver Readiness Scores / Maximum Possible Score) × 100**

---

## 1.4 Why These Four?

The selection of these four drivers is rooted in the **Burke-Litwin (1992) Causal Model of Organisational Change** and **Open Systems Theory**. An organisation is treated as an open system that interacts with:
- Its **internal subsystems** (Technology, People, Structure).
- Its **external suprasystem** (External Environment).

Any disruption in either sphere triggers the need for **adaptive**, **innovative**, or **radical change**.

---

## 1.5 Board Examiner's Framing Tip

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU ESE, when asked to "discuss the drivers of change," always start with a one-line definition, present the four drivers in a **table format**, and then explain each with at least **one real-world corporate example** (e.g., Tesla, Infosys, Amazon, or a Kerala-based case like Kerala State IT Mission) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Driver 1 – Technology

### 2.1.1 Operational Meaning
Technology, as a driver, refers to the **adoption of new tools, machinery, software, processes, and digital infrastructure** that fundamentally alters how an organisation produces, delivers, and captures value.

### 2.1.2 Why It Drives Change
- **Disruptive Innovation:** New technologies render existing business models obsolete (e.g., the shift from feature phones to smartphones dismantled Nokia's dominance).
- **Automation & Efficiency:** Reduces manual workload but demands reskilling of human capital.
- **Digital Transformation:** Cloud computing, AI/ML, IoT, and data analytics reshape decision-making.
- **Competitive Pressure:** Firms must adopt technology or lose market share to tech-savvy rivals.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Identify emerging technology relevant to industry.
2. Assess impact on current operations and workforce.
3. Pilot the technology on a small scale.
4. Scale organisation-wide with training and change management.
5. Continuously iterate based on feedback.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
Used in **Industry 4.0 adoption**, **ERP implementation** (SAP, Oracle), **e-governance projects**, and **Kerala's FibreNet initiative** for digital connectivity.

---

## 2.2 Driver 2 – People (Human Capital)

### 2.2.1 Operational Meaning
People as a driver refers to the **workforce, their skills, attitudes, motivations, leadership quality, and organisational culture** that either propel or resist change.

### 2.2.2 Why It Drives Change
- **Resistance to Change:** Employee fear, job insecurity, and skill gaps can stall any initiative.
- **Demographic Shifts:** Millennials and Gen-Z entering the workforce demand flexible, purpose-driven workplaces.
- **Leadership Transitions:** Change in top management often triggers strategic and cultural shifts.
- **Talent Scarcity:** Lack of skilled professionals forces HR-driven change (upskilling, reskilling).

### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Conduct a **skill gap analysis** to identify workforce deficiencies.
2. Design **training & development programmes** aligned with new strategic goals.
3. Address **cultural barriers** through communication, participation, and incentivisation.
4. Use **change champions** to influence peer groups.
5. Monitor engagement metrics post-change.

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility
Critical in **mergers and acquisitions** (cultural integration), **agile transformation** (team restructuring), and **DEI (Diversity, Equity, Inclusion)** initiatives.

---

## 2.3 Driver 3 – Structure

### 2.3.1 Operational Meaning
Structure refers to the **formal organisational architecture** — hierarchy, reporting lines, departmentalisation, span of control, centralisation/decentralisation, and standard operating procedures.

### 2.3.2 Why It Drives Change
- **Bureaucratic Inefficiency:** Tall hierarchies slow decision-making.
- **Scalability Needs:** Startups must move from flat to functional structures as they grow.
- **Strategic Realignment:** Moving from product-based to customer-based divisions.
- **Mergers/Demergers:** Combining two structures into a unified framework.

### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Diagnose the existing structure's strengths and limitations.
2. Choose the new structure (Functional, Divisional, Matrix, Network, Holacracy).
3. Redefine roles, responsibilities, and reporting lines.
4. Communicate the new structure to all stakeholders.
5. Re-engineer processes to fit the new design.

### 2.3.4 Real-World Utility
Used during **startup-to-corporate transitions**, **matrix implementation in MNCs**, and **flattening hierarchies in tech firms** (e.g., Valve Corporation's flat structure).

---

## 2.4 Driver 4 – External Environment (Task Environment)

### 2.4.1 Operational Meaning
The external environment encompasses **all forces outside the organisation** that influence its operations and strategy. This includes the **PESTEL** factors — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal.

### 2.4.2 Why It Drives Change
- **Globalisation:** New markets and cross-border competition.
- **Regulatory Changes:** GST in India, GDPR in Europe, environmental norms.
- **Customer Preferences:** Demand for sustainable, ethical, and personalised products.
- **Economic Shocks:** Recessions, pandemics (COVID-19), inflation.

### 2.4.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Conduct a **PESTEL analysis** to scan the macro environment.
2. Perform a **Porter's Five Forces analysis** to assess competitive intensity.
3. Identify threats and opportunities.
4. Align internal capabilities (technology, people, structure) to external demands.
5. Implement strategic change initiatives.

### 2.4.4 Real-World Utility
Used in **scenario planning**, **risk management frameworks**, and **public sector reforms** (e.g., Kerala's post-flood economic recovery strategies).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Driver | Type | Key Forces | Leadership Response | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Technology** | Internal + External | Automation, AI, Digitalisation | Invest in R&D, reskill workforce | Infosys adopting AI platforms |
| **People** | Internal | Skills, Culture, Resistance | Training, communication, participation | Google's "20% time" culture |
| **Structure** | Internal | Hierarchy, Bureaucracy | Redesign reporting, flatten hierarchy | Amazon's two-pizza team rule |
| **External Environment** | External | PESTEL, Competition, Regulation | Strategic adaptation, scenario planning | Kerala's tourism post-COVID revival |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Tip:** Always link each driver to **Kotter's 8-Step Model** or **Lewin's 3-Stage Model** in your answers to demonstrate integrated understanding of the change management process.

---

## 2.6 The Interconnectedness of the Four Drivers

The four drivers do **not operate in silos**. A change in one driver creates a **ripple effect** across the others. For example:
- Adoption of **AI technology** (Driver 1) demands **reskilling of people** (Driver 2) and **restructuring of teams** (Driver 3) to comply with **data privacy regulations** (Driver 4).

This **interdependence** is captured in the **Burke-Litwin Causal Model**, where **External Environment** is the most distal driver, and **Structure, People, and Technology** are proximal drivers influencing day-to-day operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Diagnostic Matrix

## 3.1 The Diagnostic Framework

To systematically analyse the four drivers, leaders use a **3-Layer Diagnostic Framework**:

$$
\text{Driver Impact} = f(\text{Urgency}, \text{Capacity}, \text{Resistance})
$$

Where:
- **Urgency (U):** How quickly the driver demands change (scale 1–10).
- **Capacity (C):** Organisation's ability to respond (scale 1–10).
- **Resistance (R):** Inertia or opposition to change (scale 1–10).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Process

### Step 1: Identify the Dominant Driver
Score each driver on the three parameters.

$$
\text{Driver Score} = (U \times 0.5) + (C \times 0.3) - (R \times 0.2)
$$

### Step 2: Derive the Change Readiness Index (CRI)

$$
\text{CRI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{Driver Score}_i}{40} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CRI is conceptual and is used in KTU case studies to assess organisational preparedness. A CRI above **70%** indicates high readiness; below **40%** signals the need for foundational interventions.

### Step 3: Map to a Change Strategy

$$
\text{Change Strategy} = \begin{cases} \text{Adaptive Change} & \text{if CRI} \geq 70\% \\ \text{Innovative Change} & \text{if } 40\% \leq \text{CRI} < 70\% \\ \text{Radical Change} & \text{if CRI} < 40\% \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4: Allocate Resources Using the 70-20-10 Principle

$$
\text{Resource Allocation} = \begin{cases} 70\% \text{ to People (training, culture)} \\ 20\% \text{ to Process (structure, workflows)} \\ 10\% \text{ to Technology (tools, platforms)} \end{cases}
$$

This principle, adapted from the **CCL (Center for Creative Leadership)** model, ensures that the largest investment is in human capital, since people ultimately execute any change.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: A Kerala-Based IT Firm

### Scenario
A mid-sized IT firm in Thiruvananthapuram faces:
- **Technology Driver:** New AI platform disrupting legacy systems.
- **People Driver:** 60% of workforce lacks AI skills.
- **Structure Driver:** Rigid departmental silos hinder cross-functional collaboration.
- **External Environment Driver:** New GDPR-like data protection bill in India.

### Analytical Computation

$$
U_{\text{Tech}} = 9, \quad C_{\text{Tech}} = 5, \quad R_{\text{Tech}} = 6
$$

$$
U_{\text{People}} = 8, \quad C_{\text{People}} = 4, \quad R_{\text{People}} = 7
$$

$$
U_{\text{Structure}} = 7, \quad C_{\text{Structure}} = 5, \quad R_{\text{Structure}} = 5
$$

$$
U_{\text{Env}} = 8, \quad C_{\text{Env}} = 6, \quad R_{\text{Env}} = 4
$$

#### Compute Driver Scores

$$
\text{Tech Score} = (9 \times 0.5) + (5 \times 0.3) - (6 \times 0.2) = 4.5 + 1.5 - 1.2 = 4.8
$$

$$
\text{People Score} = (8 \times 0.5) + (4 \times 0.3) - (7 \times 0.2) = 4.0 + 1.2 - 1.4 = 3.8
$$

$$
\text{Structure Score} = (7 \times 0.5) + (5 \times 0.3) - (5 \times 0.2) = 3.5 + 1.5 - 1.0 = 4.0
$$

$$
\text{Env Score} = (8 \times 0.5) + (6 \times 0.3) - (4 \times 0.2) = 4.0 + 1.8 - 0.8 = 5.0
$$

#### Compute CRI

$$
\text{CRI} = \frac{4.8 + 3.8 + 4.0 + 5.0}{40} \times 100 = \frac{17.6}{40} \times 100 = 44\%
$$

#### Interpret Result

Since CRI = **44%**, the firm falls in the **Innovative Change** band (40%–70%). The leadership must:
1. **Reskill the workforce** in AI (People – highest priority).
2. **Break departmental silos** by adopting Agile squads (Structure).
3. **Pilot AI tools** in one business unit (Technology).
4. **Establish a compliance cell** for the new data bill (External).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This analytical framework is **conceptual** and forms the basis of KTU case-study questions worth 14 marks. Students are expected to demonstrate numerical reasoning combined with qualitative judgment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term "drivers of organisational change." List the four key drivers as per standard management theory.

#### Model Answer
**Drivers of organisational change** are the internal and external forces that compel an organisation to modify its strategies, structures, processes, or culture to remain competitive and relevant.

The **four key drivers** are:
1. **Technology** – adoption of new tools and digital systems.
2. **People** – workforce skills, attitudes, and culture.
3. **Structure** – hierarchy, reporting, and workflow design.
4. **External Environment** – political, economic, social, technological, environmental, and legal forces outside the organisation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **internal** and **external** drivers of organisational change with one example each.

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Internal Drivers | External Drivers |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Within the organisation | Outside the organisation |
| **Control** | Partially controllable | Largely uncontrollable |
| **Examples** | Technology adoption, restructuring, leadership change | New regulations, economic recession, customer trends |
| **Illustration** | Infosys adopting an AI platform | GST implementation in India affecting supply chains |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four key drivers of organisational change with relevant examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the **Burke-Litwin Causal Model** links the four drivers to organisational performance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Four Key Drivers – Detailed Explanation (7 Marks)**

1. **Technology Driver:** Refers to the adoption of new tools, automation, AI, cloud computing, and digital platforms. Example: Reliance Jio disrupted the Indian telecom sector by leveraging 4G technology at disruptive pricing, forcing incumbents like Airtel and Vodafone to transform their business models.

2. **People Driver:** Encompasses the workforce's skills, attitudes, leadership, and culture. Example: Adobe Systems abolished annual performance reviews and adopted continuous feedback, transforming its people management practices and improving retention.

3. **Structure Driver:** Involves the formal architecture of hierarchy, reporting, and processes. Example: Valve Corporation operates with a flat, self-organising structure where employees choose their projects, eliminating traditional managerial layers.

4. **External Environment Driver:** Covers PESTEL factors such as government policy, economic conditions, and social trends. Example: The COVID-19 pandemic forced organisations globally to adopt remote work models, accelerating digital transformation.

**(b) Burke-Litwin Causal Model (7 Marks)**

The **Burke-Litwin (1992) Causal Model** establishes a causal chain linking **12 organisational variables** to **performance outcomes**:

- **External Environment → Mission and Strategy → Leadership → Culture → Structure → Management Practices → Work Climate → Individual Skills → Motivation → Performance.**

The model positions **External Environment** as the most distal driver, while **Technology, People, and Structure** are proximal drivers. A change in the external environment (e.g., new regulations) reshapes the mission and strategy, which demands new leadership behaviours, cultural shifts, structural redesign, and updated management practices. This cascade ultimately influences individual skills, motivation, and performance.

The model emphasises **feedback loops** — performance outcomes influence future strategy, making change a continuous, iterative process. For change leaders, the model provides a **diagnostic roadmap** to identify which variable to intervene in for maximum impact.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Analyse the **role of technology as a driver of change** in the context of Industry 4.0. Provide at least two real-world examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate how **people and structure** together act as twin drivers of organisational change. Use Kotter's model to support your analysis. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Technology and Industry 4.0 (7 Marks)**

**Industry 4.0** represents the fourth industrial revolution characterised by **cyber-physical systems, IoT, AI, big data, and additive manufacturing**. Technology as a driver of change manifests in three ways:

1. **Process Automation:** Smart factories use robotics and IoT sensors to achieve zero-defect production. Example: **Tata Consultancy Services (TCS)** deployed intelligent automation across banking processes, reducing turnaround time by 60%.

2. **Data-Driven Decision Making:** AI and analytics enable predictive insights. Example: **Flipkart** uses AI for dynamic pricing and demand forecasting, transforming supply chain management.

3. **New Business Models:** Technology enables platform-based ecosystems. Example: **Ola and Uber** created asset-light, platform-driven mobility services, displacing traditional taxi businesses.

Technology drives **adaptive change** (process upgrades), **innovative change** (new products), and **radical change** (business model disruption).

**(b) People and Structure as Twin Drivers (7 Marks)**

**People** and **Structure** are interdependent drivers. Structural redesigns are meaningless without workforce buy-in, and cultural change is difficult without formalised processes.

- **Kotter's 8-Step Model** provides a framework:
  1. **Create Urgency** – Communicate the need for change (People).
  2. **Form a Coalition** – Build a change leadership team (Structure + People).
  3. **Create Vision** – Align workforce to a shared goal (People).
  4. **Communicate Vision** – Use multiple channels (People).
  5. **Empower Action** – Remove structural barriers (Structure).
  6. **Generate Short-Term Wins** – Reward early adopters (People).
  7. **Sustain Acceleration** – Institutionalise change in structure and culture.
  8. **Anchor Changes** – Embed in organisational DNA (Structure + Culture).

**Critical Evaluation:**
- **Strengths:** Kotter's model integrates people and structure seamlessly.
- **Limitations:** The model is linear and time-consuming; it underestimates the role of **external environment** and **technology** as parallel drivers.
- **Conclusion:** Effective change requires **simultaneous intervention** in people (mindset, skills) and structure (processes, hierarchy), supported by technology and aligned with the external environment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Listing without explaining:** Simply naming the four drivers fetches only 2 out of 7 marks. Each driver must be explained with **definition + example + organisational impact**.
> 2. **Ignoring interconnection:** Treating the four drivers as silos. The board expects students to show **interdependence** (e.g., Technology → People → Structure).
> 3. **No framework linkage:** Failing to connect drivers with **Kotter's model, Lewin's model, or Burke-Litwin model** loses integration marks.
> 4. **No real-world examples:** Generic answers without corporate illustrations (TCS, Infosys, Kerala cases) are penalised.
> 5. **Skipping the CRI calculation:** In numerical/case-based questions, students often forget to compute the **Change Readiness Index**, which is a 2-mark component.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **Four Drivers:** Technology, People, Structure, External Environment.
> - **Internal vs External:** People, Structure, Technology (mostly internal); External Environment (purely external).
> - **Key Framework:** Burke-Litwin Causal Model links 12 variables; External Environment is the most distal driver.
> - **Diagnostic Tool:** Change Readiness Index (CRI) — score each driver on Urgency, Capacity, and Resistance.
> - **Change Strategy Mapping:** CRI > 70% → Adaptive; 40–70% → Innovative; < 40% → Radical.
> - **Resource Allocation:** 70% to People, 20% to Process, 10% to Technology.
> - **Integration with Other Models:** Always link drivers to **Kotter's 8-Step** or **Lewin's 3-Stage** model for full marks.
> - **Real-World Examples:** TCS (technology), Adobe (people), Valve (structure), Jio (external disruption).
> - **Kerala Context:** Mention Kerala IT Mission, FibreNet, or post-COVID tourism revival for regional relevance.
> - **Board Tip:** Use a **table** to present the four drivers; it visually impresses examiners and saves writing time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Contributing to Resistance to Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Contributing to Resistance to Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistance to Change** is defined as any behaviour or attitude on the part of an individual or group that actively opposes, delays, or obstructs the implementation of a planned organisational transformation. It is a natural, often inevitable, human and systemic response that arises when the perceived costs of change outweigh the perceived benefits, or when change threatens the **psychological contract**, **established power structures**, and **cognitive schemas** of stakeholders.

According to **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Analysis** (the conceptual foundation referenced in the KTU 2024 PESFT634 syllabus), resistance manifests as a **restraining force** that counteracts the **driving forces** pushing the organisation toward a desired future state.

$$R_{total} = f(R_{individual}, R_{organizational}, R_{environmental})$$

Where:
- $R_{total}$ = Total resistance magnitude experienced by the change agent
- $R_{individual}$ = Resistance arising from personal/psychological sources
- $R_{organizational}$ = Resistance arising from systemic/structural sources
- $R_{environmental}$ = Resistance arising from external/macro sources

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat in a Storm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine your organisation as a large ship sailing across an ocean. The **destination** is the new, improved future state. The **wind** represents the driving forces of change (vision, opportunity, growth). However, the **ocean currents, anchor chains, and crew members holding onto the rails** all act as resistance — they pull the ship back toward the old harbour. A leader's job is not to ignore these currents, but to **understand** them, **weaken** the un-constructive ones, and **redirect** the energy of the constructive ones. Resistance is therefore not the enemy — it is **feedback data** about what stakeholders truly value and fear.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Evaluations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Board Examiner (under the 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based framework) tests this topic under **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Analyse the human and structural factors that influence change adoption and develop mitigation strategies."* Expect direct 3-mark definitions, 14-mark analytical essays, and applied case-study questions worth **15 marks** in Semester-End evaluations.

### 1.4 Three Major Categories of Resistance (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 scheme consolidates resistance factors into **three distinct streams**, each of which carries dedicated weightage:

| Category | Primary Locus | KTU Cognitive Level Tested |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual-Level Resistance** | Personal psychology, habits, fear | L2 — Understand |
| **Organisational-Level Resistance** | Structure, culture, power, resources | L4 — Analyse |
| **Environmental / External Resistance** | Market, regulation, technology | L5 — Evaluate |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Resistance is **multidimensional**. It is rarely caused by a single factor — instead, it emerges from a confluence of psychological, structural, and contextual pressures. The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **Kotter & Schlesinger (1979)** and **Robbins & Judge** frameworks, which decompose resistance into clearly classifiable sources.

### 2.2 Individual-Level Factors (Personal Resistance Sources)

These originate inside the mind and personal circumstances of each employee. They are the **micro-level** causes.

#### 2.2.1 Habit
- Humans are creatures of routine. When work is automated or restructured, existing behavioural patterns are disrupted.
- **Example:** A senior accountant accustomed to manual ledgers resisting a new ERP system.

#### 2.2.2 Security
- Change often threatens perceived job security, seniority, or social standing within the group.
- **Example:** Workers fearing automation-driven layoffs.

#### 2.2.3 Economic Factors
- Concerns about personal financial loss: bonuses, overtime, perks tied to the old system.
- **Example:** Salesperson on a high-commission structure resisting a shift to a flat retainer model.

#### 2.2.4 Fear of the Unknown
- Ambiguity about future roles, expectations, or outcomes triggers cognitive anxiety.
- **Example:** Employees unsure if new reporting lines will reduce their authority.

#### 2.2.5 Selective Information Processing
- Individuals filter incoming information through existing mental models. Anything that contradicts their worldview is rejected or reinterpreted.
- **Example:** A manager interpreting the new strategy as "just another corporate fad."

#### 2.2.6 Social Factors (Peer Pressure & Group Norms)
- Resistance is amplified when an individual's social circle collectively opposes change.
- **Example:** A team of veteran employees discouraging a new hire from embracing new tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Individual factors are worth approximately **4–5 marks** in a 14-mark essay. Always connect them to a **psychological theory** (e.g., Maslow, Herzberg, or Cognitive Dissonance) to score higher.

---

### 2.3 Organisational-Level Factors (Structural Resistance Sources)

These originate from the **design, culture, and power dynamics** of the organisation itself. They are systemic and often harder to dismantle than individual resistance.

#### 2.3.1 Structural Inertia
- Organisations are designed for stability: standard operating procedures (SOPs), hierarchies, and rules create natural resistance.
- **Example:** A public-sector bank where every credit decision requires 12 levels of approval.

#### 2.3.2 Limited Focus of Change (Narrow Change Envelope)
- When only a single subsystem is changed, the surrounding subsystems resist to maintain equilibrium.
- **Example:** Introducing AI in the HR department while finance continues with legacy processes.

#### 2.3.3 Group Inertia (Groupthink & Cohesion)
- Highly cohesive groups resist change because conformity pressures outweigh individual willingness.
- **Example:** A long-tenured R&D team rejecting an open-innovation policy.

#### 2.3.4 Threat to Established Power and Influence
- Change often redistributes authority. Those losing influence will actively resist.
- **Example:** Regional managers resisting centralisation of decision-making at headquarters.

#### 2.3.5 Threat to Established Resource Allocation
- Departments fighting to protect their budgets, headcount, and facilities.
- **Example:** The marketing department resisting budget reallocation to digital initiatives.

#### 2.3.6 Organisational Culture and Legacy
- Deeply embedded values, beliefs, and rituals can render certain changes "unthinkable."
- **Example:** A family-owned firm resisting transparency reforms due to its closed culture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Organisational resistance is often **invisible** — it shows up as delays, passive non-compliance, or "we've always done it this way" statements. KTU examiners reward students who identify **latent structural resistance** in case studies.

---

### 2.4 Environmental / External Factors (Macro-Level Resistance Sources)

These arise **outside** the organisation's boundary but influence internal acceptance of change.

| External Factor | Mechanism of Resistance | KTU-Relevant Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Government Regulations** | Legal mandates may conflict with new strategic direction | GST rollout disrupting existing accounting workflows |
| **Market Competition** | Pressure to maintain competitive parity slows adoption | Firms delaying cloud migration due to vendor lock-in risks |
| **Economic Conditions** | Recession/depression curtails investment in change | Layoff freezes during downturns blocking transformation |
| **Technological Disruption** | New tech makes existing competencies obsolete | Print media resisting digital-first strategy |
| **Union & Labour Laws** | Collective bargaining slows structural reforms | Manufacturing unions resisting automation mandates |

---

### 2.6 Theoretical Linkages (For 14-Mark Depth)

$$Resistance_{intensity} \propto \frac{Perceived\ Threat}{Trust\ in\ Leadership} \times Cognitive\ Dissonance$$

Where the **perceived threat** is measured across economic, social, and psychological dimensions, and **trust in leadership** acts as the **denominator modulator**. When trust is low, even minor threats generate disproportionately large resistance.

**Real-world utility:** This conceptual formula is widely applied in **OD (Organisation Development) consulting**, **change-readiness assessments**, and **HR analytics dashboards** in firms like Deloitte, McKinsey, and Accenture.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Derivations

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of Resistance Intensity

For analytical clarity, the KTU 2024 framework models resistance as a **composite function** of three weighted dimensions. We will derive the weighted resistance score step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the Three Resistance Vectors:**

Let us define the three primary vectors of resistance as:

$$
R_I = \{Habit, Security, Economic, Fear, Perception, Social\}
$$

$$
R_O = \{Structure, Focus, Group, Power, Resource, Culture\}
$$

$$
R_E = \{Regulation, Market, Economy, Technology\}
$$

**Step 2 — Assign Weights Based on Context:**

Each vector is weighted according to the **change context** ($w_I, w_O, w_E$), where weights must satisfy the constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_I + w_O + w_E &= 1.00 \\
0 &\leq w_I, w_O, w_E \leq 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Contextual Examples:**
- A **technological change** (e.g., ERP rollout): $w_I = 0.40,\ w_O = 0.45,\ w_E = 0.15$
- A **cultural change** (e.g., DEI initiative): $w_I = 0.20,\ w_O = 0.30,\ w_E = 0.50$
- A **merger integration**: $w_I = 0.35,\ w_O = 0.50,\ w_E = 0.15$

**Step 3 — Compute Sub-Scores:**

Each factor within a vector receives a score on a 1–5 Likert scale (1 = minimal resistance, 5 = maximum resistance). The sub-score for each vector is the **arithmetic mean**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_I &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} r_{I,i}}{6} \\
S_O &= \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{6} r_{O,j}}{6} \\
S_E &= \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{4} r_{E,k}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate into Total Resistance Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= w_I \cdot S_I + w_O \cdot S_O + w_E \cdot S_E \\
\text{where } R_{total} &\in [1.0,\ 5.0]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation Banding (KTU Standard):**

| $R_{total}$ Band | Resistance Level | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| $1.0 \le R_{total} < 2.0$ | **Low Resistance** | Proceed with light interventions |
| $2.0 \le R_{total} < 3.5$ | **Moderate Resistance** | Targeted OD interventions needed |
| $3.5 \le R_{total} \le 5.0$ | **High Resistance** | Strategic overhaul or postponement |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Analysing Resistance in a Manufacturing Firm

**Scenario:** A Kerala-based textile firm (Kerala Looms Pvt. Ltd.) is implementing an Industry 4.0 smart-factory transformation. The change agent conducts a resistance assessment.

**Step 1 — Factor Scoring (out of 5):**

| Factor | Score | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Habit ($r_{I,1}$) | **4** | Workers trained on manual looms for 15+ years |
| Security ($r_{I,2}$) | **5** | Strong fear of layoff due to automation |
| Economic ($r_{I,3}$) | **4** | Piece-rate wages may drop with machine-paced work |
| Fear of Unknown ($r_{I,4}$) | **4** | No digital literacy among floor staff |
| Perception ($r_{I,5}$) | **3** | Mid-managers moderately receptive |
| Social ($r_{I,6}$) | **4** | Strong union presence amplifies peer resistance |
| Structure ($r_{O,1}$) | **5** | Highly hierarchical, 8 approval layers |
| Focus ($r_{O,2}$) | **3** | Only production line being upgraded |
| Group ($r_{O,3}$) | **5** | Long-tenured worker unions |
| Power ($r_{O,4}$) | **4** | Floor supervisors losing manual-control authority |
| Resource ($r_{O,5}$) | **3** | Budget disputes with marketing department |
| Culture ($r_{O,6}$) | **4** | "Craftsmanship pride" culture resists automation |
| Regulation ($r_{E,1}$) | **2** | No regulatory hindrance |
| Market ($r_{E,2}$) | **4** | Competition pressure (high) |
| Economy ($r_{E,3}$) | **3** | Moderate inflation pressure |
| Technology ($r_{E,4}$) | **4** | Skill gap is severe |

**Step 2 — Compute Sub-Scores:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_I &= \frac{4+5+4+4+3+4}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00 \\
S_O &= \frac{5+3+5+4+3+4}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00 \\
S_E &= \frac{2+4+3+4}{4} = \frac{13}{4} = 3.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Contextual Weights (technology-driven change: $w_I = 0.40, w_O = 0.45, w_E = 0.15$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= (0.40 \times 4.00) + (0.45 \times 4.00) + (0.15 \times 3.25) \\
R_{total} &= 1.60 + 1.80 + 0.4875 \\
R_{total} &= 3.8875 \approx 3.89
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $3.5 \le 3.89 \le 5.0$, the firm falls in the **High Resistance** band. The change agent must deploy **strategic OD interventions**, including massive re-skilling programs, union engagement workshops, transparent career-pathway redesigns, and a phased rollout across **all departments simultaneously** (to address limited-focus resistance).

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Resistance Across Three Industries

| Industry Context | Dominant Resistance Type | Most Powerful Lever | Reference Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IT / Software Firms** | Individual (fear of skill obsolescence) | Continuous learning culture | Infosys' Lex platform |
| **Manufacturing** | Organisational (union-led group inertia) | Collective bargaining & co-design | Tata Steel's digitalisation |
| **Banking / Finance** | Environmental (regulatory compliance) | Top-down regulatory alignment | SBI's YONO platform |
| **Healthcare** | Individual + Organisational (cultural) | Empathy-led communication | AIIMS digital health records |
| **Education** | Organisational (legacy culture) | Faculty co-creation models | NEP 2020 implementation |

**Interpretation of Block Diagram:**

| Block | Functional Role | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Change Initiative** | Trigger event (merger, tech adoption, restructuring) | Lightning strike |
| **B — Perception** | How stakeholders interpret the change | The prism that bends light |
| **C — Evaluation** | Cost-benefit analysis at personal level | Mental weighing scale |
| **D — Decision** | Commit or resist (active vs passive) | The crossroads |
| **E — Behavioural Response** | Open opposition, passive non-compliance, or sabotage | The action taken |
| **F — Outcome Feedback** | Consequences observed (success/failure of change) | The echo |
| **G — Adjustment Loop** | Re-evaluation based on outcomes | Self-correcting thermostat |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This loop aligns with **Lewin's three-stage model** (Unfreeze → Change → Refreeze). The feedback loop is the **refreezing** mechanism — without it, resistance resurges after initial change.
