INVISIBLE - Unconscious beliefs, deep drivers] Level1 -->|Observed By| Observer[External Stakeholder] Level2 -->|Stated By| Manager[Senior Management] Level3 -->|Held By| Everyone[Every Member] Level3 -.->|Drives| Level2 Level2 -.->|Manifests As| Level1 ``` ## 4.4 Diagnostic Decision Tree — "Which Culture Is It?" ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[Start: Analyze Organization] --> Q1{Where does power reside?} Q1 -->|One Central Figure| Power[Power Culture] Q1 -->|Position or Title| Role[Role Culture] Q1 -->|Whoever Has Expertise| Task[Task Culture] Q1 -->|The Individual Members| Person[Person Culture] Power --> Q2{Is the environment stable?} Role --> Q3{Is decision-making rule-driven?} Task --> Q4{Are projects temporary?} Person --> Q5{Is the org structured around individuals?} Q2 -->|No, turbulent| ConfP[Confirm: Power fits turbulent market] Q3 -->|Yes, SOP-driven| ConfR[Confirm: Role fits bureaucracy] Q4 -->|Yes| ConfT[Confirm: Task fits project-based work] Q5 -->|Yes| ConfPe[Confirm: Person fits expert collectives] ``` ## 4.5 Culture → Leadership → Change Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Environment] -->|Shapes| B[Culture Type] B -->|Demands| C[Leadership Style] C -->|Drives or Blocks| D[Change Outcome] D -->|Feedback| A B -.->|Mismatch with C| E[Resistance and Friction] C -.->|Mismatch with B| E ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term "Organizational Culture" and list Charles Handy's four culture types.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Organizational Culture is the set of shared assumptions, values, beliefs, norms, and behavioral patterns that guide behavior within an organization. It is the unwritten "how we do things around here." > > **Handy's Four Types (2 marks):** > 1. **Power Culture** — centralized around one strong leader; web/club symbol. > 2. **Role Culture** — rule-based, position-driven; Greek temple symbol. > 3. **Task Culture** — project-based, expertise-driven; net symbol. > 4. **Person Culture** — individual-centric, autonomous; atom cluster symbol. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Distinguish between a Strong Culture and a Weak Culture with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Strong Culture** (1.5 marks): Widely shared and intensely held values that strongly guide behavior. Change is resisted. *Example:* Infosys's culture of process discipline under Narayana Murthy. > > **Weak Culture** (1.5 marks): Loosely shared values, dependence on formal rules, inconsistent behavior. *Example:* A typical government department with frequent transfers and no common values. > > **Key distinction:** Strong cultures provide informal control and high commitment; weak cultures rely on formal procedures and exhibit low commitment. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Understanding the type of culture an organization has is the first step to leading it effectively and managing change in it."** **(a)** Explain **Charles Handy's four types of organizational cultures** with their symbols, power centers, and suitability. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss how an **understanding of culture type** influences a leader's choice of **change management strategy**. Illustrate with suitable examples. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Introduction (1 mark):** Charles Handy (1976, 1978, 1993) classified organizational cultures into four types based on the distribution of power, the basis of authority, and the orientation of the organization toward tasks and people. **The Four Types (5 marks — distribute 1.25 marks per type):** **1. Power Culture:** - **Symbol:** Web or club radiating from a central point. - **Power Center:** One dominant individual (founder, leader). - **Control Mechanism:** Personal charisma, trust, political influence. - **Suitability:** Small entrepreneurial firms, startups, crisis units. - **Strength:** Speed of decisions. **Weakness:** Founder-dependence, low scalability. **2. Role Culture:** - **Symbol:** Greek temple with strong pillars (functions). - **Power Center:** Position in hierarchy (job descriptions). - **Control Mechanism:** Rules, SOPs, formal authority. - **Suitability:** Government departments, banks, large PSUs. - **Strength:** Predictability, scalability. **Weakness:** Slow, bureaucratic, change-resistant. **3. Task Culture:** - **Symbol:** Net with knots at project intersections. - **Power Center:** Whoever holds the relevant expertise for the task. - **Control Mechanism:** Competence and task outcomes. - **Suitability:** R&D labs, consulting firms, project-based engineering teams. - **Strength:** Innovation, flexibility. **Weakness:** Power conflicts between functional and project heads. **4. Person Culture:** - **Symbol:** Cluster of atoms — separate but loosely bonded. - **Power Center:** The individual expert. - **Control Mechanism:** Mutual consent and autonomy. - **Suitability:** Medical practices, law firms, artistic collectives. - **Strength:** High autonomy. **Weakness:** No strategic direction, hard to scale. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Most organizations have a **dominant** culture but exhibit elements of all four. The leader's task is to recognize the dominant type and adapt their style accordingly. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Introduction (1 mark):** Change management strategies must align with the existing culture; otherwise, resistance is inevitable. The leader must diagnose the culture first, then choose a matching strategy. **Strategy-Culture Mapping (4 marks — distribute 1 mark per pair):** | Culture Type | Suitable Change Strategy | Example | |---|---|---| | **Power Culture** | **Top-down directive** — leader announces change with authority; fast rollout. | A startup founder mandating a new product line. | | **Role Culture** | **Bureaucratic / procedural reform** — change via revised SOPs, circulars, training. | RBI's gradual introduction of digital banking guidelines. | | **Task Culture** | **Pilot projects + expert-led innovation** — small experiments that scale. | ISRO's iterative satellite development cycles. | | **Person Culture** | **Consensus and dialogue** — co-creation with the members. | A law firm's partners agreeing on a new compensation model. | **Cross-cultural Challenges (1 mark):** Leaders must often manage *subcultures* and *counter-cultures*. For instance, in a Role-dominant PSU, the R&D subculture may be Task-oriented; the leader must engage both differently. **Conclusion (1 mark):** A culturally intelligent leader diagnoses first, then adapts the change strategy. **Kotter's 8-step model** and **Lewin's 3-step model** (unfreeze-change-refreeze) work best when culture-fit is considered. --- ### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative to Question A): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Describe the **Competing Values Framework (CVF)** by Cameron and Quinn. Identify its **four culture types** with examples. (7 marks) **(b)** Critically analyze the relationship between **organizational culture, leadership style, and change resistance**. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Introduction to CVF (2 marks):** Cameron and Quinn (1999, *"Diagnosing and Changing Organizational Culture"*) built a 2×2 matrix on two value dimensions: - **Vertical axis:** Flexibility & Discretion vs. Stability & Control. - **Horizontal axis:** Internal Focus & Integration vs. External Focus & Differentiation. This yields **four culture archetypes**. **The Four Cultures (4 marks — distribute 1 mark each):** 1. **Clan Culture** (Internal + Flexible): Like an extended family. Emphasizes loyalty, tradition, mentorship. *Example:* A traditional Indian family business like Wipro under Azim Premji's early era. 2. **Adhocracy Culture** (External + Flexible): Dynamic, entrepreneurial, innovation-driven. *Example:* Google, Tesla, 3M. 3. **Market Culture** (External + Stable): Results-oriented, competitive, goal-driven. *Example:* Amway, classic sales-driven organizations. 4. **Hierarchy Culture** (Internal + Stable): Structured, controlled, process-driven. *Example:* Indian Railways, government secretariats. **Diagnostic Tool (1 mark):** The CVF is operationalized through the **Organizational Culture Assessment Instrument (OCAI)** — a 24-item questionnaire scoring the four types out of 100 points. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **The Triadic Relationship (1 mark):** Culture, leadership, and change resistance are interlocked. Culture determines what counts as legitimate leadership; mismatched leadership triggers resistance to change. **Analytical Breakdown (4 marks):** **1. Culture influences leadership legitimacy:** In a Role Culture, only an *administrative* leader is accepted. A charismatic visionary will be seen as illegitimate. **2. Leadership reinforces (or changes) culture:** Schein shows that leaders are the *primary transmitters* of culture through their behavior. A new CEO can shift culture over time (e.g., Satya Nadella transforming Microsoft from a Hierarchy to a Learning/Adhocracy culture). **3. Mismatch causes change resistance:** $$ \text{Resistance Intensity} = f(\text{Culture-Change Mismatch}, \text{Culture Strength}) $$ A strong Hierarchy Culture will violently resist an Adhocracy-style disruption. **Critical View (1 mark):** Cultures are not destiny; strong leadership *can* shift cultures, but the deeper the level (Schein's assumptions), the harder the shift. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Effective leaders **diagnose culture → match style → design culturally-fit change** to minimize resistance and maximize success. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Types of Cultures" Questions:** > > 1. **Mere listing without symbols** — Always draw/mention the **iconic symbols** (Web, Temple, Net, Atom). Examiners allocate 0.5–1 mark specifically for symbols. > 2. **No real-world examples** — Generic answers score less. Mention **Tata, ISRO, Infosys, Google, Indian Railways** etc. > 3. **Confusing Handy with Cameron & Quinn** — They are *two different frameworks*. Do not mix the four types of Handy with the four types of CVF in one paragraph without distinguishing. > 4. **Ignoring Schein's depth** — When asked about *changing* culture, surface-only answers (just "change slogans") fail. Use **Schein's three levels** to show depth. > 5. **Forgetting the Culture-Leadership-Change link** — KTU Module 4 tests **integration**. An answer that discusses culture *without* linking to leadership or change loses marks. > 6. **No diagrams** — A simple hand-drawn figure (Web/Temple/Net/Atom OR CVF matrix) earns **at least 1–2 bonus marks** in valuation. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Bank — Module 4: Types of Cultures** - ✅ **Charles Handy** is the **single most important name** in this module — memorize his four types, symbols, and suitability. - ✅ The **four Handy types** are: **Power, Role, Task, Person** — remember as **"P-R-T-P"** (Power, Role, Task, Person). - ✅ **Symbols:** Web (Power), Temple (Role), Net (Task), Atom Cluster (Person). - ✅ **Cameron & Quinn's CVF** has four quadrants: **Clan, Adhocracy, Market, Hierarchy**. - ✅ **OCAI** is the diagnostic instrument for CVF — mention it for full marks. - ✅ **Schein's three levels** (Artifacts → Espoused Values → Assumptions) are key for any *change-related* sub-question. - ✅ **Strong Culture ≠ Always Good** — toxic strong cultures (e.g., Enron) can destroy value. - ✅ **Subcultures** exist within dominant cultures and can drive innovation or conflict. - ✅ **Culture shapes leadership legitimacy** — mismatched leaders are rejected regardless of competence. - ✅ **Change strategies must be culture-fit**: - Power/Market → Directive top-down - Role/Hierarchy → Bureaucratic reform - Task/Adhocracy → Pilot projects - Person/Clan → Consensus - ✅ Always include **at least one real corporate example** (Tata, Infosys, Google, ISRO, Indian Railways, Reliance). - ✅ **Always draw a diagram** — Handy's four icons OR the CVF 2×2 matrix. - ✅ KTU examiners reward **integration** — link culture + leadership + change in every answer. - ✅ Avoid mixing **Handy's** types with **Cameron & Quinn's** types as if they are the same — they overlap conceptually but use different dimensions. --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Types of Cultures | PESFT634 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Ways to Influence Culture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Ways to Influence Culture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Organizational Culture** is a pattern of shared basic assumptions that the group learned as it solved its problems of external adaptation and internal integration, which has worked well enough to be considered valid and, therefore, to be taught to new members as the correct way to perceive, think, and feel in relation to those problems.

**Influence Mechanisms** are the conscious and unconscious actions, signals, and structures through which leaders embed, transmit, and reinforce the desired cultural assumptions across the organization. Edgar Schein (Sloan School, MIT) classified these mechanisms into two distinct tiers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Embedding Mechanisms** answer the question: *"What do leaders DO directly to create culture?"*
>
> **Secondary Articulation and Reinforcement Mechanisms** answer: *"What structural and symbolic tools do leaders use to AMPLIFY and STABILIZE the culture?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Greenhouse and Soil" Analogy**
>
> Think of organizational culture as a **plant growing in a greenhouse**.
> - The **Primary Mechanisms** are the **gardener's direct actions** — what the gardener waters, what plants they prune, which seeds they plant, and which weeds they pull out. The gardener is **hands-on, deliberate, and immediate**.
> - The **Secondary Mechanisms** are the **greenhouse structure itself** — the soil composition, the ventilation system, the walls, the labelling on the pots, and the stories told to apprentice gardeners. These structures **reinforce and stabilize** the gardener's direct work over the long term.
>
> Without primary mechanisms, the gardener does nothing. Without secondary mechanisms, the gardener's work gets washed away by the next rain. **Both are essential.**

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

- It is a **direct exam-favourite** from the Leadership and Managing Change module.
- Students are frequently asked to **list, distinguish, and provide examples** of both mechanism types.
- It maps directly to **Course Outcome CO4** (Analyze mechanisms to lead organizational change).
- Bloom's Level tested: **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** predominantly.

---

## 1.4 The Two-Tier Framework at a Glance

| Tier | Focus | Time Horizon | Leader's Role |
|:-----|:------|:-------------|:--------------|
| **Primary** | Daily, observable behavior | Short-term, immediate | Active doer |
| **Secondary** | Structure, symbols, systems | Long-term, cumulative | Architect of reinforcement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Embedding Mechanisms (6 Levers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **direct, behavioral, day-to-day actions** of leaders that send loud signals to employees about what truly matters. **What leaders DO speaks louder than what leaders SAY.**

### Lever 1 — What Leaders Pay Attention To, Measure, and Control
- Leaders signal priorities through their **attention**.
- Whatever a leader **measures** becomes important; whatever is ignored is treated as trivial.
- *Example:* A CEO who personally reviews customer complaint logs (and not just revenue dashboards) signals that customers matter more than numbers.

### Lever 2 — Leader Reactions to Critical Incidents and Crises
- Crises are **culture-defining moments**.
- How a leader *behaves under pressure* is scrutinized intensely.
- *Example:* A leader who lays off thousands during a crisis but keeps their own bonus teaches a culture of *selfishness at the top*. Conversely, taking a pay cut first teaches *shared sacrifice*.

### Lever 3 — Observed Criteria for Resource Allocation
- Money, talent, and time are **scarce resources**.
- Where a leader **spends** these resources reveals true values.
- *Example:* Allocating 60% of the innovation budget to a futuristic moonshot project reveals a culture of *bold experimentation*, even if the annual report claims "fiscal discipline."

### Lever 4 — Deliberate Role Modeling, Teaching, and Coaching
- Leaders are **walking curricula**.
- Mentoring style, dress code, work hours, communication tone — all are taught implicitly.
- *Example:* A senior partner who stays late and replies to junior emails at midnight teaches that *the firm values sacrifice and responsiveness*.

### Lever 5 — Observed Criteria for Rewards and Status
- Who gets **promoted, celebrated, and rewarded** defines the aspirational culture.
- *Example:* Promoting the employee who bullied a competitor to hit a quota teaches *Machiavellian victory*. Promoting the mentor who uplifted a junior teaches *collaborative growth*.

### Lever 6 — Observed Criteria for Recruitment, Selection, Promotion, Retirement, and Excommunication
- **Hiring and firing** are the most powerful cultural gatekeepers.
- "Hire for culture-add, not just culture-fit" is a modern reframing.
- *Example:* Exiting a top performer who violated the code of conduct teaches that *no one is above the rules*.

---

## 2.2 Secondary Articulation and Reinforcement Mechanisms (6 Amplifiers)

> [!NOTE]
> These mechanisms **do not create culture directly**. They **encode, broadcast, and stabilize** the culture established by primary mechanisms. They are the *infrastructure* of culture.

### Amplifier 1 — Organizational Design and Structure
- Hierarchy, span of control, centralization, and reporting lines **embody** values.
- *Flat structure* signals *empowerment*; *tall bureaucracy* signals *control and order*.

### Amplifier 2 — Systems and Procedures
- HR policies, performance appraisal forms, IT workflows.
- *Example:* A 360-degree feedback system embeds a culture of *mutual accountability*.

### Amplifier 3 — Design of Physical Space, Facades, and Buildings
- Office layout (open vs. cubicles), art on walls, location of CEO's desk.
- *Example:* An open-door policy reinforced by an *actual open physical door* and a reception-less entrance.

### Amplifier 4 — Stories, Legends, Myths, and Parables
- Narratives about *founding heroes*, *near-death experiences*, or *famous victories*.
- *Example:* The legendary tale of an employee who shipped a product overnight to save a customer — retold at every onboarding.

### Amplifier 5 — Statements of Organizational Philosophy, Creeds, and Charters
- Mission statements, vision documents, charters.
- These are **weak alone** but **powerful when consistent** with primary behavior.

### Amplifier 6 — Formal Statements of Organizational Values and Codes of Conduct
- Published value frameworks, ethical codes.
- *Example:* A "Code of Honor" pinned in every conference room reinforces the value of integrity.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Primary Mechanism | Key Verb | Quick Cue Word |
|:-:|:------------------|:---------|:---------------|
| 1 | Attention / Measurement / Control | *Focus on* | **ATTENTION** |
| 2 | Reaction to Crises | *React to* | **CRISIS** |
| 3 | Resource Allocation | *Spend on* | **RESOURCES** |
| 4 | Role Modeling / Coaching | *Behave like* | **MODEL** |
| 5 | Rewards and Status | *Celebrate* | **REWARDS** |
| 6 | Recruitment & Excommunication | *Hire / Fire* | **HIRING** |

| # | Secondary Mechanism | Type | Quick Cue Word |
|:-:|:--------------------|:-----|:---------------|
| 1 | Organizational Design | Structural | **STRUCTURE** |
| 2 | Systems and Procedures | Structural | **SYSTEMS** |
| 3 | Physical Space | Symbolic | **SPACE** |
| 4 | Stories and Legends | Symbolic | **STORIES** |
| 5 | Philosophy / Charters | Symbolic | **CREED** |
| 6 | Codes of Conduct | Symbolic | **CODE** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Primary — "A C R R M H"** → *Attention, Crisis, Resources, Rewards, Modeling, Hiring*
> **Mnemonic for Secondary — "D S S S C C"** → *Design, Systems, Space, Stories, Creed, Code*

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

- **Startup Founders:** Use primary mechanisms heavily in the first 100 days to imprint culture.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions:** Leaders deliberately *excommunicate* acquired-firm leaders to signal whose culture will dominate.
- **Public Sector Engineers:** The hierarchical Indian engineering bureaucracy is an example of a *structure-based* secondary mechanism shaping behavior.
- **Tech Giants (TCS, Infosys, Google):** Use onboarding *stories* and *physical campus design* as secondary reinforcement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Application Framework

Since this is a management/leadership topic (not a numerical engineering derivation), the "analytical matrix" here is a **systematic decision framework** that a KTU student can apply to any case study to score full marks.

## 3.1 The 5-Step Diagnostic Framework

### Step 1 — Identify the Cultural Outcome
> **Logic:** Before analyzing influence, identify *what* cultural assumption is being created or reinforced.
- Read the scenario carefully.
- Is the desired culture: *innovative, customer-centric, frugal, hierarchical, learning-oriented*?

### Step 2 — Map Observed Leader Behavior to Primary Mechanisms
> **Logic:** For each primary lever (1–6), ask: *"Is the leader in the case study demonstrating this lever?"*
- *Yes* → It is a primary mechanism in action.
- *No* → Note it as a gap.

### Step 3 — Map Structural / Symbolic Elements to Secondary Mechanisms
> **Logic:** Look for organizational architecture — policies, buildings, rituals, mission statements.
- Classify each as one of the six secondary amplifiers.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Consistency
> **Logic:** A *strong* culture emerges when primary behavior and secondary structures **align**. A *weak or toxic* culture emerges when they **contradict**.
- *Inconsistency Test:* "Does the CEO preach humility but occupy a 5,000 sq. ft. corner office?" → *Contradiction → Weak culture signal.*

### Step 5 — Recommend the Counter-Mechanism
> **Logic:** If the primary lever is missing, the leader should *introduce* it. If the secondary amplifier contradicts the primary behavior, the leader should *realign* the structure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Analytical Walkthrough

> **Case Study (KTU-style scenario):**
> *"Apoorva is the new VP of Engineering at BharatTech. She notices that despite the company values poster in every cabin reading 'Innovation and Risk-Taking,' engineers are afraid to propose new ideas. Senior leaders publicly criticize any failed project, and the bonus structure rewards only on-time, on-budget delivery."*

### Solution Walkthrough:

**Step 1 — Identify Cultural Outcome:**
- *Stated Value:* Innovation and risk-taking.
- *Actual Outcome (Observed):* Risk-aversion and blame-culture.

**Step 2 — Map Primary Mechanisms (6-Lever Audit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lever 1 (Attention)} &: \text{Senior leaders publicly critique failures} \Rightarrow \text{Anti-innovation signal} \\
\text{Lever 2 (Crisis)} &: \text{No data given} \Rightarrow \text{Cannot evaluate} \\
\text{Lever 3 (Resources)} &: \text{Bonus funds flow only to on-budget projects} \Rightarrow \text{Anti-innovation} \\
\text{Lever 4 (Modeling)} &: \text{Senior leaders themselves avoid risk} \Rightarrow \text{Hypocrisy signal} \\
\text{Lever 5 (Rewards)} &: \text{Bonus = on-time, on-budget only} \Rightarrow \text{Punishes experimentation} \\
\text{Lever 6 (Hiring)} &: \text{No data given} \Rightarrow \text{Cannot evaluate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Map Secondary Mechanisms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amplifier 5 (Creed)} &: \text{Values poster on wall says 'Innovation'} \\
\text{Amplifier 6 (Code)} &: \text{No specific risk-taking code mentioned}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Consistency:**

$$
\text{Creed} = \text{Innovation} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Reality} = \text{Risk-aversion} \Rightarrow \text{STRONG CONTRADICTION}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommend Counter-Mechanisms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)} \quad &\text{Reframe Bonus Structure: } 30\% \text{ weight to "learning from bold experiments."} \\
\text{(b)} \quad &\text{Leader Reaction to Failure: Replace public criticism with a "Failure Forum."} \\
\text{(c)} \quad &\text{Secondary Reinforcement: Update the Code of Conduct to explicitly protect risk-takers.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 5-step framework is the EXACT structure KTU examiners expect** in 14-mark case-study questions. Memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Decision Matrix

| Situation | Use Primary Mechanism? | Use Secondary Mechanism? |
|:----------|:----------------------|:-------------------------|
| **New leader taking charge** | ✅ Yes — strong | ⏳ Build gradually |
| **Crisis / Turnaround** | ✅ Yes — critical | ⚠️ Audit for misalignments |
| **Mature, stable culture** | ⚠️ Light touch | ✅ Yes — reinforce deeply |
| **Mergers / Acquisitions** | ✅ Yes — symbolic acts | ✅ Yes — design harmonization |
| **Toxic culture transformation** | ✅ Yes — public excommunication | ✅ Yes — overhaul systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Schein Cultural Influence Framework (Hierarchical Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LEADER["ORGANIZATIONAL LEADER"]
    
    LEADER --> P["PRIMARY EMBEDDING MECHANISMS"]
    LEADER --> S["SECONDARY REINFORCEMENT MECHANISMS"]
    LEADER --> C["ORGANIZATIONAL CULTURE OUTPUT"]
    
    P --> P1["1. Attention, Measurement, Control"]
    P --> P2["2. Reaction to Crises"]
    P --> P3["3. Resource Allocation"]
    P --> P4["4. Role Modeling and Coaching"]
    P --> P5["5. Rewards and Status"]
    P --> P6["6. Recruitment and Excommunication"]
    
    S --> S1["1. Organizational Design"]
    S --> S2["2. Systems and Procedures"]
    S --> S3["3. Physical Space and Facades"]
    S --> S4["4. Stories, Legends, Myths"]
    S --> S5["5. Philosophy and Charters"]
    S --> S6["6. Formal Values and Codes"]
    
    P1 --> C
    P2 --> C
    P3 --> C
    P4 --> C
    P5 --> C
    P6 --> C
    S1 --> C
    S2 --> C
    S3 --> C
    S4 --> C
    S5 --> C
    S6 --> C
    
    C --> OUT["SHARED ASSUMPTIONS, BELIEFS, BEHAVIOR NORMS"]
```

## 4.2 Consistency Test — Aligned vs. Contradictory Culture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ALIGNED["ALIGNED CULTURE - STRONG"]
        A1["Primary: CEO rewards risk-takers"] 
        A2["Secondary: Code protects risk-takers"]
        A1 ==> A2
    end
    
    subgraph CONTRADICT["CONTRADICTORY CULTURE - WEAK"]
        B1["Primary: CEO publicly shames failures"]
        B2["Secondary: Poster says 'Innovate Boldly'"]
        B1 -.-> B2
    end
```

## 4.3 Leader's Action Flow During Culture Change

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Leader Inherits or Desires New Culture"]) 
    START --> STEP1["STEP 1: Define Target Cultural Assumption"]
    STEP1 --> STEP2["STEP 2: Apply Primary Mechanisms"]
    STEP2 --> SUB1["Audit Attention, Crises, Resources"]
    SUB1 --> SUB2["Adjust Modeling, Rewards, Hiring"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["STEP 3: Apply Secondary Mechanisms"]
    STEP3 --> SUB3["Redesign Structure, Systems, Space"]
    SUB3 --> SUB4["Refresh Stories, Creed, Codes"]
    STEP3 --> STEP4["STEP 4: Consistency Audit"]
    STEP4 --> CHECK{"Primary = Secondary?"}
    CHECK -- YES --> SUCCESS(["EMBEDDED CULTURE"])
    CHECK -- NO --> STEP2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between primary and secondary mechanisms of influencing culture. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Mechanisms | Secondary Mechanisms |
|:-------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| Nature | Direct, behavioral actions of leader | Structural and symbolic reinforcements |
| Time horizon | Immediate, observable | Long-term, cumulative |
| Source of influence | Leader's personal behavior | Organizational systems, symbols, and artifacts |
| Example | CEO publicly praises a bold experiment that failed | Company displays a framed photo of the founder in every office |

*Primary mechanisms are WHAT leaders DO directly; secondary mechanisms are HOW the organization REINFORCES what leaders do.*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Why is "leader reaction to a crisis" considered one of the most powerful primary embedding mechanisms?**

**Model Answer:**
A crisis is a *high-visibility, high-stakes* moment during which employees closely observe the leader's behavior to learn what is *truly* valued. Statements of policy or values are temporarily suspended; only **actions matter**. When a leader during a crisis demonstrates courage, fairness, sacrifice, or transparency, employees infer that these are the real cultural assumptions. Conversely, indecisive, selfish, or hypocritical behavior during a crisis permanently damages the leader's credibility. Hence, crisis reactions are the most concentrated, unambiguous cultural signal a leader can send.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Identify and explain any **four primary embedding mechanisms** that a leader can use to shape organizational culture. Illustrate each with a real-world example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"A CEO publicly insists that employees should 'fail fast and learn faster,' yet the performance appraisal system penalizes any project marked as 'failed.' Discuss the cultural impact of this contradiction and recommend a corrective course of action."*

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** Primary embedding mechanisms are direct, behavioral actions of leaders that signal cultural priorities to the organization.

**Mechanism 1 — Attention, Measurement, and Control (1.5 marks):**
A leader who personally reviews the *innovation pipeline dashboard* weekly — rather than the *revenue dashboard alone* — embeds the assumption that *innovation* is a top cultural priority. Attention is the scarcest resource of a leader, and where it flows, the organization follows.
*Example:* Steve Jobs personally reviewed product design mockups weekly, embedding a culture of *design excellence* at Apple.

**Mechanism 2 — Reaction to Critical Incidents (1.5 marks):**
A crisis reveals the leader's true values. When a data breach occurred at a tech firm, the CEO who immediately accepted responsibility, communicated transparently, and resigned bonus eligibility embedded a culture of *accountability and trust*.

**Mechanism 3 — Rewards and Status Allocation (2 marks):**
The "Employee of the Month" trophy, promotion decisions, and bonus letters are public declarations of values. A company that celebrates the *mentor who lifted three juniors* over the *individual genius* embeds *collaboration over heroism*.
*Example:* Infosys's "Spot Award" program publicly recognizes peer-led learning.

**Mechanism 4 — Recruitment and Excommunication (1 mark):**
Hiring a Chief Culture Officer with a people-first background, while respectfully exiting a top performer who bullied subordinates, embeds the assumption that *respect is non-negotiable*.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks

**Diagnosis (2 marks):**
This is a classic case of **primary-secondary misalignment**. The CEO's verbal message is a *secondary* signal (statement of value), while the appraisal system is a *secondary structural mechanism*. Both contradict the actual primary behavior implied by the appraisal. The resulting cultural impact is:

1. *Toxic hypocrisy* — employees learn to ignore posters and listen only to systems.
2. *Risk-aversion* — the dominant cultural assumption becomes "stay invisible, never fail."
3. *Loss of psychological safety* — innovation is a euphemism for "career suicide."

**Corrective Recommendations (5 marks — 1 mark each + 1 for structure):**

| # | Recommendation | Mechanism Type |
|:-:|:---------------|:---------------|
| 1 | Reframe the appraisal: allocate 30% weight to "learning outcomes from experiments" | Secondary (System) |
| 2 | CEO must publicly celebrate a *failed-but-instructive* experiment and reward the lessons | Primary (Reaction/Modeling) |
| 3 | Revise the Code of Conduct to explicitly *protect* ethical risk-takers from penalty | Secondary (Code) |
| 4 | Establish a "Failure Forum" — a quarterly ritual where teams share lessons from abandoned projects | Secondary (Story/Ritual) |
| 5 | Re-train managers to coach experimentation rather than audit compliance | Primary (Modeling) |

**Conclusion (0 mark, but adds polish):** A leader's words are weak; their *systems* and *reactions* are the real culture.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six secondary articulation and reinforcement mechanisms** through which culture is stabilized. For each, give an example relevant to a software engineering organization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Analyze the case: *"A newly appointed CTO at a public-sector engineering firm wants to shift the culture from 'process compliance' to 'engineering excellence.' Identify which three primary and three secondary mechanisms she should deploy in the first 90 days."*

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks (1 mark per mechanism + 0.5 mark example each)

| # | Secondary Mechanism | Explanation | Software-Org Example |
|:-:|:--------------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Organizational Design** | Reporting structure and span of control embody values. | Move from 8-layer hierarchy to flat squads of 6–8 developers (Spotify model). |
| 2 | **Systems and Procedures** | Workflows and HR processes encode norms. | Implement pair-programming and code review as *mandatory* workflow steps. |
| 3 | **Physical Space** | Architecture, layout, and facades reflect culture. | Open-plan "innovation bays" with whiteboard walls, no private cabins for ICs. |
| 4 | **Stories, Legends, Myths** | Narratives transmit unwritten values across generations. | Onboarding session tells the legend of the engineer who caught a critical bug at 3 AM and saved a launch. |
| 5 | **Philosophy, Charters, Creeds** | Formal statements of purpose. | The "Engineering Charter" pinned in every team's wiki — e.g., Google's "Code is Law." |
| 6 | **Values and Codes of Conduct** | Published ethical standards. | A "Code of Craft" requiring unit tests, documentation, and zero-tolerance for shortcuts. |

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks (3 marks for 3 primary + 3 marks for 3 secondary + 1 mark for structure)

**Primary Mechanisms to Deploy in 90 Days (1 mark each):**

1. **Attention (Lever 1):** CTO personally attends and *closes* a code review on the first 30 days. *Cultural signal:* "The craft matters more than paperwork."
2. **Reaction to Crisis (Lever 2):** When a project slips, publicly thank the team for raising the risk early, instead of punishing. *Cultural signal:* "Honesty is rewarded, surprises are not."
3. **Role Modeling (Lever 4):** CTO herself codes alongside the team for 2 hours every Friday. *Cultural signal:* "Leaders are craftsmen first."

**Secondary Mechanisms to Deploy in 90 Days (1 mark each):**

1. **Systems (Amplifier 2):** Replace 40-page compliance forms with a *lightweight engineering excellence checklist* in the SDLC.
2. **Physical Space (Amplifier 3):** Convert the central admin hall into an "Engineering Excellence Wall" displaying the best code commits.
3. **Stories (Amplifier 4):** Inaugurate a "Friday Story Hour" where senior engineers narrate past engineering near-misses.

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
The combination ensures that the new culture is *experienced* (primary) and *reinforced* (secondary), creating a *consistent* cultural signal across all touchpoints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students write *only lists* of mechanisms without *examples*. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **30% marks for examples**. Always pair mechanism with example.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Students confuse *primary* and *secondary* mechanisms. *Reminder:* Primary = leader's direct action; Secondary = organization's structural/symbolic reinforcement.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Students fail to perform a **consistency check** in case studies. Always evaluate whether the primary and secondary signals *agree* or *contradict*.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Writing generic answers like "leaders should be good role models." Be **specific** — *what* behavior, *when*, *where*, *to whom*.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Ignoring the **6-numbered structure**. Many students write 3 or 4 mechanisms; KTU rubric penalizes omission.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**
>
> - ✅ Culture is a **pattern of shared assumptions**, not a poster on the wall.
> - ✅ Schein's framework has **two tiers**: **Primary (6 mechanisms)** and **Secondary (6 mechanisms)**.
> - ✅ **Primary Mnemonic** — *A C R R M H* → **Attention, Crisis, Resources, Rewards, Modeling, Hiring**.
> - ✅ **Secondary Mnemonic** — *D S S S C C* → **Design, Systems, Space, Stories, Creed, Code**.
> - ✅ Primary mechanisms = WHAT leaders DO directly; Secondary mechanisms = HOW the organization AMPLIFIES.
> - ✅ Cultural **strength** comes from **consistency** between primary and secondary signals.
> - ✅ Cultural **weakness or toxicity** arises from **contradiction** (espoused values vs. lived values).
> - ✅ **Crises** are the most powerful single moment for primary mechanism deployment.
> - ✅ **Hiring and firing** are the *gatekeepers* of culture.
> - ✅ Use the **5-step framework** (Identify → Map Primary → Map Secondary → Check Consistency → Recommend) for any case study.
> - ✅ Always **illustrate with examples** — minimum one example per mechanism in a 14-mark answer.
> - ✅ Avoid generic statements — be **specific** about *behavior, time, place, and recipient*.
> - ✅ For 14-mark answers: Allocate **7 marks × 2 parts**; each part needs intro, body, example, and conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Organisational Culture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elements of Organisational Culture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Organisational Culture** refers to the *shared system of meanings, values, beliefs, assumptions, norms, and artifacts* that members of an organisation hold in common and that distinguish the organisation from other entities. As per the KTU 2024 scheme framework, it is a **socially constructed, soft-system variable** that influences every managerial decision, leadership style, and change initiative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Reference Standard (Schein, 1985; Cameron & Quinn, 2011):**
> "Organisational culture is the pattern of shared basic assumptions — invented, discovered, or developed by a given group as it learns to cope with its problems of external adaptation and internal integration — that has worked well enough to be considered valid and, therefore, to be taught to new members as the correct way to perceive, think, and feel in relation to those problems."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of organisational culture like the **"personality" of a company** — similar to how an individual human has a personality shaped by upbringing, values, and habits, a company has a culture shaped by its founders, history, and the collective behaviour of its people.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Restaurant" 🍴**
> Imagine two restaurants in the same city:
> - **Restaurant A:** Staff greet customers warmly, the owner personally trains new chefs, mistakes are openly discussed to improve, and the menu changes seasonally based on customer feedback. (Open, learning, customer-first culture)
> - **Restaurant B:** Staff are silent, the owner uses strict rules, mistakes are punished, and the menu has remained the same for 20 years. (Hierarchical, rule-bound, tradition-driven culture)
>
> Both serve food, but the **invisible rules, beliefs, and rituals** are completely different. That invisible layer **IS** the organisational culture.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU 2024

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In PESFT634, organisational culture is the *foundation* of Module 4 because:
> - Leaders cannot initiate **change** without understanding the *existing* culture.
> - Culture acts as the **"operating system"** of an organisation.
> - Module 5 (Managing Change) directly depends on a strong grasp of cultural elements.

## 1.4 The Two Functional Purposes of Culture

| **Purpose** | **Function** | **Engineering/Management Analogy** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| **External Adaptation** | Helps the firm cope with environment, customers, competition | Like a building's *foundation* adapting to soil type |
| **Internal Integration** | Binds members together into a coherent social system | Like *welding* parts into a single structure |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Schein's Three Levels of Organisational Culture (Most Critical for KTU)

The most widely tested framework. **Edgar Schein (1985)** proposed that culture operates at three distinct levels of visibility and difficulty to change.

### Level 1: Artifacts & Behaviours (Surface Level — Most Visible)
- **Definition:** The visible, tangible, and audible elements of culture.
- **Examples:** Office layout, dress code, logos, mission statements, rituals, slogans, ceremonies.
- **KTU Trick:** Easy to observe but **hard to decode** without understanding the deeper levels.

### Level 2: Espoused Values & Beliefs (Middle Level — Conscious)
- **Definition:** Strategies, goals, philosophies, and justifications articulated by members.
- **Examples:** "Customer is king," "Innovation is our lifeblood," "Quality above profit."
- **KTU Insight:** The *gap* between espoused values and actual behaviour often signals **cultural dysfunction**.

### Level 3: Basic Underlying Assumptions (Deepest Level — Unconscious)
- **Definition:** Unconscious, taken-for-granted beliefs, perceptions, thoughts, and feelings.
- **Examples:** "People are inherently lazy," "Customers always lie," "Authority must not be questioned."
- **KTU Insight:** These are the **most powerful** and **most resistant to change** because members are *unaware* of them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Pattern:** Questions often test whether students can **classify** examples into the three Schein levels. A common 3-mark question is *"Give two examples of artifacts and two of espoused values in a typical IT company."*

## 2.2 The Seven Primary Elements of Organisational Culture

Based on the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, organisational culture is composed of **seven interconnected elements**. These are the high-yield content for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 2.2.1 Innovation and Risk-Taking
- The degree to which employees are encouraged to be innovative and take calculated risks.
- **High:** Google, 3M, Tesla.
- **Low:** Traditional government banks, railway systems.

### 2.2.2 Attention to Detail
- The degree to which employees are expected to exhibit precision, analysis, and attention to detail.
- **High:** McKinsey Consulting, Six Sigma-driven manufacturing firms.
- **Low:** Creative advertising agencies.

### 2.2.3 Outcome Orientation
- The degree to which management focuses on **results** rather than on the **process** used to achieve them.
- **High:** Sales-driven companies (e.g., Amway, insurance firms).
- **Low:** Academic institutions focused on learning process.

### 2.2.4 People Orientation
- The degree to which management decisions take into account the effect on people within the organisation.
- **High:** Tata Group, Infosys (in early years), Scandinavian companies.
- **Low:** Hard-numbers-driven startups.

### 2.2.5 Team Orientation
- The degree to which work activities are organised around **teams** rather than individuals.
- **High:** Agile software companies, hospital surgical teams.
- **Low:** Sole proprietorship law firms.

### 2.2.6 Aggressiveness
- The degree to which people are aggressive and competitive rather than easygoing and cooperative.
- **High:** Investment banks, military units.
- **Low:** Monasteries, NGOs.

### 2.2.7 Stability
- The degree to which organisational activities emphasise **maintaining the status quo** in contrast to growth.
- **High:** Established public-sector companies.
- **Low:** Growth-stage startups.

## 2.3 KTU Framework Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

| **Element** | **Definition** | **High-Culture Example** | **Low-Culture Example** |
|-------------|---------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Innovation & Risk | Encouragement of new ideas | Google, 3M | Traditional PSU bank |
| Attention to Detail | Precision emphasis | Six Sigma factory | Ad agency |
| Outcome Orientation | Result vs process focus | Insurance sales | University |
| People Orientation | Employee consideration | Scandinavian firms | Hyper-growth startup |
| Team Orientation | Group-based work | Agile IT firm | Sole lawyer |
| Aggressiveness | Competitive intensity | Trading floor | NGO |
| Stability | Status-quo vs growth | Govt. department | Growth startup |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "I-A-O-P-T-A-S":**
> **I**nnovation, **A**ttention, **O**utcome, **P**eople, **T**eam, **A**ggressiveness, **S**tability.

## 2.4 Types of Culture — Cameron & Quinn's Competing Values Framework (High-Yield for 14-mark questions)

Cameron and Quinn identified **four archetypes** of organisational culture, based on two dimensions: *flexibility vs. control* and *internal focus vs. external focus*.

| **Type** | **Core Value** | **Leader Style** | **Example** |
|----------|---------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Clan Culture** | Collaboration, loyalty, tradition | Mentor, facilitator | Family-run business |
| **Adhocracy Culture** | Innovation, risk-taking, dynamism | Visionary, entrepreneur | Tesla, SpaceX |
| **Market Culture** | Results, competition, achievement | Driver, producer | Amazon, Amway |
| **Hierarchy Culture** | Structure, control, efficiency | Coordinator, monitor | Government offices, ISRO |

## 2.5 Functions of Organisational Culture (Frequently Asked 7-mark Sub-Part)

1. **Boundary-Defining Function:** Distinguishes one organisation from another.
2. **Sense-Making Function:** Provides members with a frame of reference for understanding organisational events.
3. **Commitment Function:** Generates organisational commitment beyond rational self-interest.
4. **Social System Stabilisation:** Acts as the social glue binding members.
5. **Control Function:** Shapes and controls member behaviour through norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Classification Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Context:** Since this is a management topic, mathematical derivations are not applicable. Instead, we perform **structured conceptual derivation** — building the cultural model step by step with the same level of rigour as a derivation.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation: From "Group" to "Culture" (Schein's Causal Chain)

The formation of organisational culture follows a deterministic, stage-by-stage cognitive process. Below is the exhaustive logical derivation.

### Step 1: Founding Trigger Event
A founder (or small founding group) faces a critical **problem of external adaptation** (e.g., survival, market fit) or **internal integration** (e.g., coordination, communication).

$$\text{Trigger Event} \rightarrow \text{Initial Action} \rightarrow \text{Outcome Feedback}$$

### Step 2: Repeated Success Reinforcement
If the initial action produces a *positive* outcome, the founder's group **repeats** this action, because the team has learned that the behaviour works.

$$\text{Repeated Positive Outcome} \rightarrow \text{Behaviour Becomes Routine}$$

### Step 3: Value Articulation
Over time, the group members develop a **shared mental model** of *why* the action worked. They articulate this as a *value* or *belief*.

$$\text{Belief} = f(\text{Repeated Routine}) \rightarrow \text{Espoused Value}$$

### Step 4: Assumption Internalisation
Eventually, the value becomes so deeply embedded that members *stop questioning* it. The belief becomes a **taken-for-granted assumption**, and any deviation causes anxiety.

$$\text{Unquestioned Belief} \rightarrow \text{Underlying Assumption} \rightarrow \text{Defended Against Contradiction}$$

### Step 5: Cultural Transmission
New members are socialised into the assumption, ensuring **inter-generational continuity** of the culture.

$$\text{New Member Entry} \rightarrow \text{Socialisation} \rightarrow \text{Assumption Adoption}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Check:** A complete answer on cultural elements must always link the **artifact → value → assumption** hierarchy. Skipping levels is the most common reason for losing 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Classification Matrix: Mapping Examples to Schein's Levels

| **Observable Example** | **Schein Level** | **Reasoning** |
|------------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Open-plan office with bean bags | Artifact (Level 1) | Visible, tangible |
| "Innovate or die" poster on wall | Artifact (Level 1) | Physical slogan |
| Founder's repeated statement: "We are family" | Espoused Value (Level 2) | Verbalised belief |
| Manager yelling at employee for being late | Behavior → may reflect underlying assumption |
| New employee feeling *guilty* for not staying late | Underlying Assumption (Level 3) | Unconscious norm |
| "Quality over speed" repeatedly enforced | Espoused Value (Level 2) | Expressed philosophy |

## 3.3 Diagnosing Culture: The Sathe & Davidson Framework (Analytical)

$$\text{Cultural Diagnosis} = \text{Observation of Artifacts} + \text{Analysis of Values} + \text{Uncovering of Assumptions}$$

| **Diagnostic Step** | **Method** | **Output** |
|---------------------|-----------|------------|
| Step A: Observe | Ethnography, observation, walk-throughs | Inventory of artifacts |
| Step B: Interview | In-depth employee interviews | List of espoused values |
| Step C: Triangulate | Cross-check espoused values vs. actual behaviour | Identified gaps & dysfunction |
| Step D: Excavate | Deep, probing interviews about history | Underlying assumptions |

## 3.4 Strong vs. Weak Culture Analytical Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Strong Culture** | **Weak Culture** |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Shared Values** | Wide, deep, intense | Few, ambiguous |
| **Employee Commitment** | High, beyond self-interest | Low, calculative |
| **Behaviour Predictability** | High — culture controls conduct | Low — relies on rules |
| **Change Resistance** | Very high (rigid) | Low (flexible but possibly directionless) |
| **Performance Link** | Strong link to high performance (when values align) | Weak or negative link |
| **KTU Example** | Infosys under Narayan Murthy | Struggling mid-tier IT firm |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]
**"Define organisational culture. List any four of its primary elements."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Organisational culture is the system of shared assumptions, values, beliefs, and norms that govern behaviour in an organisation (Schein, 1985).
- **Four primary elements (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. Innovation and risk-taking
  2. Attention to detail
  3. Outcome orientation
  4. People orientation
  5. Team orientation
  6. Aggressiveness
  7. Stability

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always *define first*, then *list* with *examples* to gain the full 3 marks. Avoid giving only a list without definition — you will lose 1 mark.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**"Distinguish between artifacts and espoused values with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Artifacts (1.5 marks)** | **Espoused Values (1.5 marks)** |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Visible, tangible, physical elements | Verbalised, conscious, strategic |
| Easy to observe | May not reflect actual behaviour |
| *Example:* Open office, dress code, logo | *Example:* "Quality is our priority" |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks — Module 4 Standard Pattern]

> **"a) Explain Schein's three levels of organisational culture with examples. (7 marks)**
> **b) Discuss the seven primary elements of organisational culture as identified in the KTU 2024 framework. (7 marks)"**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Schein's Three Levels (7 Marks)

Schein's framework distinguishes culture at three levels of visibility and resistance to change:

**Level 1 — Artifacts and Behaviours (2 marks):**
The most visible layer. Includes physical environment, dress code, logos, ceremonies, and rituals. *Example:* The Zen-garden campus of Apple, the "blue shirt" uniform of Infosys.

**Level 2 — Espoused Values (2 marks):**
The conscious, articulated strategies, philosophies, and goals. *Example:* Google's "10x thinking" philosophy, Toyota's "Kaizen" — continuous improvement.

**Level 3 — Basic Underlying Assumptions (3 marks):**
The deepest, unconscious, taken-for-granted beliefs. These are the hardest to change because members are *unaware* of them. *Example:* A hospital's assumption that "patient lives are above profits" — explaining why a doctor will not discharge a critical patient for cost reasons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write all three levels with examples but **fail to discuss the cause-and-effect link** between them. To secure full marks, you must explain *how artifacts reflect values* and *how values stem from assumptions*. (Loss of 2 marks otherwise.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Seven Primary Elements (7 Marks)

| **Element** | **Meaning** | **Mark Allocation** |
|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Innovation & Risk | Degree of new-idea encouragement | 1 mark |
| Attention to Detail | Precision, analysis emphasis | 1 mark |
| Outcome Orientation | Results vs process focus | 1 mark |
| People Orientation | Employee consideration in decisions | 1 mark |
| Team Orientation | Group vs individual work | 1 mark |
| Aggressiveness | Competitive intensity | 1 mark |
| Stability | Status-quo vs growth orientation | 1 mark |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Reasons:**
> 1. *Missing one element* (–1 mark)
> 2. *No real-world example* (–1 mark)
> 3. *Confusing "people orientation" with "team orientation"* (–1 mark)

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

> **"a) Explain the Cameron and Quinn Competing Values Framework. How does it help in diagnosing organisational culture? (7 marks)**
> **b) Critically examine the functions and dysfunctions of a strong organisational culture. (7 marks)"**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Cameron & Quinn Framework (7 Marks)

The framework classifies culture into four types based on two dimensions: *flexibility vs. control* and *internal vs. external focus* (4 marks for the four types, 2 marks for diagnostic use, 1 mark for example).

- **Clan Culture:** Internal + Flexible. Family-like, collaborative. *Example:* Family restaurant.
- **Adhocracy Culture:** External + Flexible. Innovative, risk-taking. *Example:* SpaceX.
- **Market Culture:** External + Control. Results-driven. *Example:* Amazon warehouse.
- **Hierarchy Culture:** Internal + Control. Structured, rule-bound. *Example:* Indian Railways.

**Diagnostic Utility (2 marks):** Using the OCAI (Organisational Culture Assessment Instrument), managers plot scores to identify dominant culture and gaps between *current* and *preferred* culture.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Strong Culture — Functions vs Dysfunctions (7 Marks)

| **Functions (3.5 marks)** | **Dysfunctions (3.5 marks)** |
|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Provides behavioural stability | Hinders adaptability |
| Strong employee identification | Resistance to change |
| Reduces ambiguity in decisions | Encourages groupthink |
| Acts as internal control system | Barrier to diversity & inclusion |
| Attracts/retrains aligned employees | Reinforces status-quo bias |
| Example: Tata's value-driven culture | Example: Kodak's culture of film refused to embrace digital |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Thinking Required:** For full 7 marks, a *balanced* answer must present *both* positive and negative implications. A one-sided argument scores only 4–5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Schein's 3 Levels:** Artifacts (visible) → Espoused Values (verbalised) → Underlying Assumptions (unconscious).
- [ ] **Seven Elements Mnemonic:** **I-A-O-P-T-A-S** (Innovation, Attention, Outcome, People, Team, Aggressiveness, Stability).
- [ ] **Cameron & Quinn's 4 Cultures:** Clan, Adhocracy, Market, Hierarchy.
- [ ] **Two Purposes of Culture:** External Adaptation + Internal Integration.
- [ ] **Strong Culture** = high shared values, high commitment, high behavioural predictability, but **low change-readiness**.
- [ ] **Founder's Role:** Culture typically originates from a single founder's vision and is reinforced by repeated success.
- [ ] **Artifacts vs Values:** A gap between the two is the most common sign of cultural dysfunction.
- [ ] **KTU High-Yield:** Always structure cultural analysis using **Schein's 3 levels** as the backbone.
- [ ] **Critical Assumption:** Underlying assumptions are the *most stable* and *hardest to change* — critical for Module 5 (Change Management).
- [ ] **Avoid Confusing:** *Espoused values* ≠ *Assumptions* (the former is spoken; the latter is unspoken).
- [ ] **Number the elements** in the exam for clean mark allocation.
- [ ] **Always cite Schein (1985)** or **Cameron & Quinn (2011)** in definitions to gain the methodology mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** In any 14-mark question on this topic, the formula for high marks is:
> **Definition (2) + Schein's 3 Levels (4) + 7 Elements (4) + Examples (2) + Diagram/Framework Reference (2) = 14 marks.**
> Always close with a 1–2 line conclusion linking culture to **leadership and change** (this satisfies the PESFT634 course philosophy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT634 - LEADERSHIP AND MANAGING CHANGE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diagnosing Organisational Culture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Organisational Culture** is the collective sum of the *values, beliefs, assumptions, symbols, artefacts, and behavioural patterns* that an organisation has developed over time in response to its internal integration and external adaptation challenges. According to **Edgar Schein (1985)**, culture can be defined as:

> *"A pattern of shared basic assumptions that the group learned as it solved its problems of external adaptation and internal integration, that has worked well enough to be considered valid and, therefore, to be taught to new members as the correct way to perceive, think, and feel in relation to those problems."*

**Diagnosing Organisational Culture** is the systematic process of uncovering, interpreting, and evaluating the deep-seated assumptions, values, and behavioural norms operating within an organisation. It is a **diagnostic exercise** that allows leaders to identify the gap between the *espoused* (stated) culture and the *enacted* (actual) culture, and to determine whether the existing culture supports or hinders strategic goals and change initiatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Diagnosing culture is the **pre-requisite step** before initiating any change intervention. As per the syllabus, leaders must understand "what is" before designing "what should be." This topic is a **favourite for KTU ESE** (End Semester Examination) and is frequently asked as a 14-mark application question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Iceberg of Culture"

Imagine organisational culture as a **giant iceberg floating in the ocean**:

- The **visible tip** above water represents the **Artefacts and Behaviours** — dress code, office layout, logos, slogans, rituals, ceremonies. Anyone can see these, but they are easy to mimic.
- The **middle layer** just below the waterline represents the **Espoused Values and Norms** — stated mission, vision, strategies, philosophies. These are often documented but may not match reality.
- The **massive, hidden base** deep underwater represents the **Underlying Assumptions** — unconscious beliefs, mental models, deeply embedded perceptions about truth, time, human nature, and the environment. This is the *real* culture and the hardest to change.

**Diagnosing culture is essentially sending down a submarine** to inspect what lies beneath the surface.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Terms in KTU 2024 Context

| Standard Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Subculture** | A smaller culture within a larger organisation, often defined by function or geography |
| **Dominant Culture** | The core values shared by the majority of organisational members |
| **Counter-culture** | A subculture whose values sharply oppose the dominant culture |
| **Cultural Fit (Person-Organisation Fit)** | The degree of alignment between employee values and organisational values |
| **Cultural Web** | A 6-element diagnostic model by Johnson & Scholes |
| **CVF** | Competing Values Framework by Cameron & Quinn |
| **OCAI** | Organisational Culture Assessment Instrument |

> [!NOTE]
> The **diagnostic phase** typically consumes **30% to 40%** of any change management project's timeline (per Kotter's 8-step model). Skipping this phase is the **#1 reason** change initiatives fail — a fact KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Schein's Three Levels of Organisational Culture (The Foundational Model)

Edgar Schein's model is the **gold standard** referenced in KTU 2024 syllabus. The diagnostic must uncover all three levels:

### Level 1: Artefacts and Creations (Visible, Tangible)
- **What you see:** Office layout, dress code, logos, mission statement posters, technology used, rituals, ceremonies, language/jargon, stories told.
- **Diagnostic ease:** Very easy to observe.
- **Diagnostic challenge:** Easy to copy or misrepresent (cultural façade).

### Level 2: Espoused Beliefs and Values (Stated Ideals)
- **What is declared:** Vision, mission, strategic plans, operating philosophies, codes of conduct, values posters.
- **Diagnostic ease:** Easy to find in annual reports, intranet.
- **Diagnostic challenge:** Often disconnected from actual behaviour. The **gap between espoused and enacted** is a critical diagnostic finding.

### Level 3: Basic Underlying Assumptions (Unconscious, Taken-for-Granted)
- **What is believed:** Deeply embedded beliefs about human nature, relationships with environment, truth, time, the nature of human activity.
- **Diagnostic ease:** Very hard — requires deep qualitative methods.
- **Diagnostic challenge:** Members cannot easily articulate these; they are pre-conscious.

## 2.2 The Competing Values Framework (CVF) — Cameron & Quinn

The **CVF** is the most widely used **quantitative diagnostic tool**. It classifies culture along two dimensions, creating four quadrants:

### Axis 1: Internal Focus & Integration vs. External Focus & Differentiation
### Axis 2: Flexibility & Discretion vs. Stability & Control

| Culture Type | Focus | Core Values | Dominant Traits | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Clan Culture** | Internal, Flexible | Collaboration, Loyalty, Tradition | Mentor-like leadership, family feel, high cohesion | Start-ups, NGOs, family businesses |
| **Adhocracy Culture** | External, Flexible | Innovation, Risk-taking, Dynamism | Entrepreneurial, visionary leadership, experimentation | Tech firms, R&D, creative agencies |
| **Market Culture** | External, Stable | Results, Competition, Achievement | Hard-driving, goal-oriented, market-share focused | Sales-driven firms, FMCG |
| **Hierarchy Culture** | Internal, Stable | Order, Structure, Control, Efficiency | Procedural, formal leadership, bureaucracy | Government, banks, manufacturing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Cameron & Quinn's **OCAI (Organisational Culture Assessment Instrument)** uses 6 key dimensions scored against the four culture types. The "Now" and "Preferred" scores are plotted to reveal the **culture gap** — this gap is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

## 2.3 The Cultural Web (Johnson & Scholes) — KTU Favourite

The **Cultural Web** is a **6-element diagnostic framework** used to map the taken-for-granted assumptions in an organisation. It is **highly testable** in KTU exams.

| Element | Definition | Diagnostic Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Stories** | Narratives told repeatedly about past events | What stories do people tell newcomers? |
| **Rituals & Routines** | Daily behaviours and ceremonies that signal acceptable conduct | What behaviours get rewarded? |
| **Symbols** | Logos, dress, titles, status markers | What symbols signal power? |
| **Organisational Structure** | Reporting lines, hierarchy, communication channels | Is the structure flat or tall? |
| **Control Systems** | Performance metrics, reward systems, budgeting | What gets measured? |
| **Power Structures** | The most influential individuals or groups | Who really makes decisions? |

> [!NOTE]
> The **paradigm** (the central assumption) sits at the centre of the Cultural Web. The **6 elements are the symptoms** of the paradigm. Diagnosis involves identifying both the symptoms and the root paradigm.

## 2.4 Denison's Model of Organisational Culture

Denison's model is built on **four cultural traits** linked to organisational effectiveness:

1. **Mission** — strategic direction and intent
2. **Consistency** — coherence, agreement, core values
3. **Involvement** — empowerment, team orientation, capability development
4. **Adaptability** — customer focus, organisational learning, change orientation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each trait is measured by **3 indices**, giving 12 sub-indicators. KTU examiners often ask: *"Which trait is most predictive of long-term financial performance?"* The answer is **Consistency** (per Denison's research).

## 2.5 Cultural Diagnostic Methods — A Comprehensive List

| Method | Type | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Survey / Questionnaire (e.g., OCAI)** | Quantitative | Large sample, statistical validity | Surface-level, response bias |
| **Semi-structured Interviews** | Qualitative | Deep, contextual | Time-consuming, hard to scale |
| **Direct Observation / Shadowing** | Ethnographic | Reveals actual behaviour | Observer effect, Hawthorne effect |
| **Document Analysis** | Archival | Objective, historical | May not reflect current reality |
| **Focus Groups** | Qualitative | Group dynamics reveal norms | Dominant voices may bias |
| **Organisational Climate Survey** | Quantitative | Captures employee perception | Distinguishes climate from culture poorly |
| **Storytelling / Narrative Analysis** | Qualitative | Reveals underlying assumptions | Subjective interpretation |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Diagnostic Frameworks

| Framework | Purpose | Key Output | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Schein's 3 Levels | Uncover deep assumptions | Layered cultural profile | Very High |
| CVF (Cameron & Quinn) | Classify dominant culture | Quadrant score + culture gap | Very High |
| Cultural Web (Johnson & Scholes) | Map cultural elements | 6-element audit | High |
| Denison Model | Link culture to performance | 12-trait scorecard | Medium |
| Hofstede 6-D Model | National-cultural diagnosis | Dimension scores | Medium |
| GLOBE Study | Cross-cultural leadership | Cultural clusters | Low-Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> **Hofstede's model is for NATIONAL culture, not organisational culture.** Many KTU students confuse the two. Be very careful in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Cultural Diagnosis Process

## 3.1 The 5-Step Cultural Diagnostic Process (KTU Model Answer Structure)

A complete cultural diagnosis follows this **5-step analytical sequence**. KTU examiners award full marks only when students demonstrate this structured approach:

### **Step 1: Define the Diagnostic Purpose and Scope**
- Clarify **why** the diagnosis is being conducted (pre-change assessment, merger integration, M&A due diligence, performance problem).
- Define the **scope** — whole organisation, specific unit, or sub-team.
- Identify the **stakeholders** — leadership, employees, customers, suppliers.

### **Step 2: Select Appropriate Diagnostic Tools**
- Use a **mixed-methods approach** (quantitative + qualitative) to triangulate findings.
- Quantitative: OCAI, Denison Survey, custom Likert-scale questionnaire.
- Qualitative: Interviews, focus groups, observation, document analysis.

### **Step 3: Data Collection Across Schein's Three Levels**
The diagnostic must collect evidence at **all three levels** to avoid surface-level conclusions:

- **Level 1 (Artefacts):** Observe the physical environment, conduct document analysis of published values, map rituals.
- **Level 2 (Espoused Values):** Interview senior leaders, review strategic plans and vision statements.
- **Level 3 (Underlying Assumptions):** Conduct deep interviews, analyse recurring stories, use participant observation.

### **Step 4: Data Analysis and Interpretation**
- **Triangulate** findings across data sources to ensure validity.
- Identify **patterns, themes, and anomalies**.
- Map findings onto a chosen framework (e.g., CVF, Cultural Web).
- Quantify the **culture gap** (the difference between "current" and "preferred" culture).

### **Step 5: Report Findings and Recommend Action**
- Present a **cultural profile** to leadership.
- Highlight the **gap between espoused and enacted** culture.
- Recommend **levers for change** (e.g., structural, symbolic, behavioural).
- Establish **cultural KPIs** to monitor ongoing cultural health.

## 3.2 Worked Example — The Culture Gap Calculation (OCAI Method)

A medium-sized IT firm uses the **OCAI** to diagnose its culture. Six dimensions are scored (0–100) across the four culture types (Clan, Adhocracy, Market, Hierarchy). The total for each quadrant must sum to **100 points**.

### Sample Scoring Across All Six Dimensions

| OCAI Dimension | Clan (Now) | Adhocracy (Now) | Market (Now) | Hierarchy (Now) | Sum |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dominant Characteristics | 30 | 25 | 20 | 25 | 100 |
| Organisational Glue | 35 | 20 | 15 | 30 | 100 |
| Leadership Style | 30 | 25 | 20 | 25 | 100 |
| Strategic Emphasis | 25 | 30 | 25 | 20 | 100 |
| Criteria of Success | 25 | 25 | 30 | 20 | 100 |
| Management Style | 30 | 25 | 20 | 25 | 100 |
| **Average Score (Now)** | **29.17** | **25.00** | **21.67** | **24.17** | **100** |

### Interpretation Logic

- **Dominant Culture (Now):** Clan (29.17) — the firm is currently a *people-oriented, family-like* organisation.
- **Weakest Culture (Now):** Market (21.67) — less results-driven.
- **Preferred Culture (as per senior management, post-diagnosis):** Adhocracy (38) — they want innovation-led.

### The Culture Gap Equation

$$
\text{Culture Gap}_{i} \;=\; \vert \text{Preferred Score}_{i} - \text{Current Score}_{i} \vert
$$

where $i$ denotes each of the four culture types (Clan, Adhocracy, Market, Hierarchy).

### Step-by-Step Calculation for Each Quadrant

$$
\text{Culture Gap}_{\text{Clan}} \;=\; \vert 15 - 29.17 \vert \;=\; 14.17
$$

$$
\text{Culture Gap}_{\text{Adhocracy}} \;=\; \vert 38 - 25.00 \vert \;=\; 13.00
$$

$$
\text{Culture Gap}_{\text{Market}} \;=\; \vert 27 - 21.67 \vert \;=\; 5.33
$$

$$
\text{Culture Gap}_{\text{Hierarchy}} \;=\; \vert 20 - 24.17 \vert \;=\; 4.17
$$

### Aggregate Culture Gap (Total Transformation Required)

$$
\text{Total Culture Gap} \;=\; 14.17 + 13.00 + 5.33 + 4.17 \;=\; 36.67
$$

### Diagnostic Conclusion

- The firm needs to **reduce Clan emphasis by 14.17 points** and **increase Adhocracy emphasis by 13 points**.
- A total transformation load of **36.67 points** indicates a **major cultural shift** is required — Kotter's change model would take **3 to 5 years**.
- **Recommendation:** Use Kotter's 8-step model with a *guiding coalition* and *short-term wins* to sustain the change.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Diagnostic Tool

| Diagnostic Need | Best Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Quick classification of dominant culture | **OCAI (CVF)** | Simple, visual quadrant mapping |
| Understand deep unconscious assumptions | **Schein 3-Level Interview Protocol** | Probes beneath artefacts |
| Map taken-for-granted assumptions | **Cultural Web (Johnson & Scholes)** | 6-element audit structure |
| Link culture to financial performance | **Denison Model** | Empirically validated KPI linkage |
| Diagnose national-cultural mix (M&A) | **Hofstede / GLOBE** | Cross-cultural comparison |
| Identify culture-fit issues in M&A | **Galpin's Human Side of Mergers** | Specific merger diagnostic |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-step process is the universally accepted KTU model answer structure** for any 14-mark question on "Diagnosing Organisational Culture." Memorise and reproduce it for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
